Hardship customers without access to solar PV will pay around 60% more for each unit of passthrough of CfD cost recovery than customers with solar PV (assuming they continue to be passed through on the volumetric component of network charges). 6. Discussion Based on our findings, the impact of costs of CfDs

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: ased on our findings, the impact of costs of CfDs recovered by network charges on different customer groups could be viewed as a regressive form of taxation used by state governments to fund renewable energy commitments. We discuss the implications of our findings in the following sections. 6.1. Emerging social equity issues We build on existing CfD research to show that this policy mecha- nism is

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: D research to show that this policy mecha- nism is inequitable and disproportionately affects vulnerable hardship customers who tend to have higher energy usage (Nelson et al., 2019; Simshauser and Nelson, 2014). Policy makers ought to consider our findings in the context of the policy being implemented. Specifically, our research reveals that when CfD costs are distributed through variable use ch

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: CfD costs are distributed through variable use charges, hardship customers end up shouldering 30% more of the associated costs compared to standard households. This disparity is a result of their higher energy consumption. Conversely, we have demonstrated that implementing an average fixed rate of $56.80 would distribute costs evenly (though not equitably) among all cus- tomers. CfD charges are d

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: quitably) among all cus- tomers. CfD charges are designed as such to implicitly function as a regressive form of taxation. To establish a more equitable solution, it would be more appropriate to adopt a framework similar to that of the progressive Australian taxation system.4 One such model would have low-income hardship customers earning $18,201 – $45,000 per annum pay a contribution toward publi

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: $45,000 per annum pay a contribution toward public policy of 19c for each $1 over $18,200 earned while conversely high income individuals earning $180,001 and over per annum pay 45c for each $1 over $180,000 (Nelson et al., 2019; Simshauser and Nelson, 2014). While these taxation costs would be spread across public spending (e.g., health and education as well as renewable energy initiatives) they

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: tion as well as renewable energy initiatives) they illustrate the proportional difference between this scenario and the CfD approach where hardship customers pay a greater or equal contribution to renewable energy policy. We also show that fixed subsidies and underwriting policies like CfDs prioritises profits over social equity. CfDs aim to reduce the cost of capital for energy generators, and th

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: the cost of capital for energy generators, and this approach is inequitable when policy costs are recovered through variable charges, given the higher consumption patterns of hardship and vulnerable consumers. This becomes even more concerning as these communities are also likely to bear the brunt of the expected mitigation costs (Nelson et al., 2012). In Fig. 1, we highlight the presence of regi

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: 012). In Fig. 1, we highlight the presence of regional inequity in both scenarios (variable and fixed charges) regarding the distribution of CfD costs across Local Government Areas (LGAs). In the variable scenario, LGAs with low socioeconomic status, such as the Hunter Valley region (which will be particularly affected by the transition to renewable en- ergy), bear the highest proportion of these

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: e en- ergy), bear the highest proportion of these costs. Conversely, in the fixed scenario, there is a narrower spread of outcomes across all LGAs and the communities within the broader Hunter Valley region are more evenly dispersed through the results. From the perspective of social equity it is equally important that policymakers take into account the disparities in energy consumption and income

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: t the disparities in energy consumption and income across the NSW AusGrid geographical 4 Australian taxation rates are as follows: 0 – $18,200 Nil; $18,201 – $45,000, 19c for each $1 over $18,200; $45,001 – $120,000; $5092 plus 32.5c for each $1 over $45,000; $120,001 – $180,000, $29,467 plus 37c for each $1 over $120,000; $180,001 and over $51,667 plus 45c for each $1 over $180,000. 4 T. Nelson a

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: plus 45c for each $1 over $180,000. 4 T. Nelson and T. Dodd network when designing and implementing renewable energy policies. In our analysis we also demonstrate that households have the po- tential to mitigate their exposure to CfD costs through the adoption of solar PV. This presents an alternative policy approach to address the inequalities highlighted concerning hardship customers and LGA Cf

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: ghlighted concerning hardship customers and LGA CfD contributions. Existing literature indicates that low-income households and regions tend to have lower solar PV adoption rates, making it un- likely for them to avoid CfD payments by this means without additional policy interventions (Chester, 2014; Dodd and Nelson, 2022; Simshauser and Nelson, 2014). The literature identifies various barriers to

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: 14). The literature identifies various barriers to solar PV adoption, with two prominent ones being limited access to capital for installation and the requirement of homeownership, which ensures a return on investment and the right to install the system (Chester, 2015; Tidemann et al., 2019). Considering these factors, policymakers could explore strategies to accelerate the uptake of solar PV amon

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: rategies to accelerate the uptake of solar PV among hardship and low-income consumers, aiming to reduce costs associated with CfDs. By facilitating the adoption of solar PV among low-income house- holds, policymakers can help alleviate their financial strain and reduce their dependence on traditional energy sources. Research studies have consistently shown that low-income households in Australia f

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: ly shown that low-income households in Australia face a greater proportion of their weekly income being allocated towards en- ergy costs when compared to higher-income households (Independent Pricing and Regulatory Tribunal, 2015; Nelson et al., 2019; Simshauser et al., 2011). Factors such as housing conditions, household composi- tion, and individual circumstances significantly influence househol

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: ual circumstances significantly influence household energy consumption, with higher-income households generally consuming more electricity (Boeri et al., 2020; Dodd et al., 2020). Vulnerable low-income households, particularly those with more chil- dren, lack of solar PV installations, and either renting or paying off a mortgage, are at a higher risk of facing financial hardship due to energy bill

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: sk of facing financial hardship due to energy bills (Nelson et al., 2019). In order to address these challenges and mitigate the burden of CfD payments, it is crucial to ensure that these disadvantaged households have access to a more equitable CfD cost mechanism and/or solar energy solutions. 6.2. Emerging recommendations Based on the abovementioned implications and discussions of our findings we

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: ed implications and discussions of our findings we deduce three recommendations to support greater equity related to CfDs as summarized in the following section. 6.2.1. Policy recommendation 1: shifting CfD costs to government balance sheets If governments choose to persist with CfD-style policies, it would be advisable to fund CfD costs through government balance sheets rather than network tariff

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: vernment balance sheets rather than network tariffs. In Australia, taxation revenue is progressively collected from individual taxpayers, making it likely that the impacts on households would be equitable if these costs were recovered through government funding. This approach is particularly crucial when considering regional impacts and the incidence of cost recovery. Concentrated impacts of clima

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: ce of cost recovery. Concentrated impacts of climate mitigation policies in specific geographic regions, such as the Hunter Valley, could be further exac- erbated if CfD costs are recovered through network charges, leading to an additional burden on these communities already facing the brunt of climate change mitigation economic impacts. 3 To check the consistency of the data provided, the authors

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: the consistency of the data provided, the authors contrasted the annual kWh consumption figures with those provided by the Australian Energy Regulator. The figures are relatively consistent – see page 25 for solar PV and non-solar PV consumption figures: https://www.aer.gov.au/system/files/Resi dential%20energy%20consumption%20benchmarks%20-%209%20December %202020_0.pdf, Accessed online on 9 Octo

text: 0December %202020_0.pdf, Accessed online on 9 October 2022. 5 E n e r g y P o l ic y 183(2023)113829 6.2.2. Policy recommendation 2: incorporating fixed network charges in cost recovery The majority of Australian households have network tariffs struc- tured with two parts: fixed charges (daily) and variable charges (per kilowatt-hour). This structure creates inherent cross subsidies among differen

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: re creates inherent cross subsidies among different customer cohorts. Recovering CfD policy costs through network charges effectively magnifies these inequitable outcomes for consumers. Consideration ought to be given regarding how tariff designs that recover CfD costs (if kept) impact social equity. For example, our findings show that greater use of the fixed charge would be one way in which a mo

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: of the fixed charge would be one way in which a more equitable distribution of costs could occur. However, there are other means to enhance equity as now explored. 6.2.3. Policy recommendation 3: pivot policy support for solar PV toward low-income and hardship customers In policy recommendation 3, we suggest that if CfD cost recovery continues to be achieved via network tariffs, priority be given

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: e achieved via network tariffs, priority be given to providing low-income individuals with access to solar PV. Recent studies, such as the one by Dodd and Nelson (2022), have demonstrated that solar PV is economically viable for the average household. Urgent re-evaluation of solar PV subsidies, like the Australian Government’s Small-scale Renewable Energy Scheme (SRES), is recommended to assess th

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: Energy Scheme (SRES), is recommended to assess their necessity, effectiveness, efficiency, and efficacy. As high- lighted by the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC, 2022), these policies are not only regressively funded but also result in lower overall retail tariffs for solar PV households. Our analysis in the previous section establishes that installing solar PV significantly

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: stablishes that installing solar PV significantly reduces energy bills. When CfD policy costs are recov- ered through network charges, this effect is accentuated. Hence, poli- cymakers should consult earlier recommendations, such as those by Dodd and Nelson (2022), to redirect policy support for solar PV instal- lation toward low-income rental households and hardship customers (also see Tidemann e

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: eholds and hardship customers (also see Tidemann et al., 2019). Current policies lack adequate focus on spatial distribution, and support for solar PV installation could be better targeted at customers facing barriers to installation, such as renters who encounter difficulties due to split incentives. 6.3. Limitations and areas for future research Our study has some limitations that highlight dire

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: Our study has some limitations that highlight directions for future research. First, the findings of our study are based on a specific sample of hardship and non-hardship customer accounts from Australia in the year 2021. While we made efforts to create average profiles and capture diverse energy usage patterns, the generalisability of our findings may be restricted to the specific sample and time

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: may be restricted to the specific sample and time period we examined. To enhance the applicability of the research, future studies could expand the model to assess the implications of Contract for Difference (CfD) mechanisms in different contexts. Second, our modelling process relied on various assumptions and simplifications to estimate the cost impacts and distribution of CfD cost recovery. The

title: Contracts-for-Difference: An assessment of social equity considerations in the renewable energy transition
text: impacts and distribution of CfD cost recovery. These models may not fully capture the complexities and nuances of real-world energy consumption patterns, network charges, and renewable energy policies. Specifically, when analysing the impact of CfD cost recovery on different socio-economic communities, we used average electricity consumption and median income data at the Local Government Area (LGA

Introduction With the increasing urgency of a transition away from fossil

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: creasing urgency of a transition away from fossil fuels, the discussion of various options of low-carbon energy technologies has attracted the interest of the wider society. Public engagement in energy policy debates is motivated by climate concerns, but also by rising en- ergy prices and the relevance of energy independence, especially following the Russian invasion of Ukraine (Osiˇcka and Cˇ ern

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: he Russian invasion of Ukraine (Osiˇcka and Cˇ ernoch, 2022). The energy preferences in society are, however, not only guided by economic and environmental dimensions, but also by different beliefs and values. Investments and expansions of various energy technologies can therefore provoke different socio-psychological responses (Dermont et al., 2017; Batel, 2020), and be subject to political polar

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: 7; Batel, 2020), and be subject to political polarization (Hawes and Nowlin, 2022). Energy systems are accordingly not just physical infrastructures, but socio-technical systems, entangled with economic relations, power structures, social practices, norms, and institutions (Parag and Sovacool, 2016; Newell et al., 2022). The social dimensions of energy systems have attracted the interest of resear

text: ergy systems have attracted the interest of researchers, with a particular focus on the concept of social accep- tance of different energy technologies (Batel et al., 2013; Fast, 2013). text: elopment of renewable energy, particularly wind power, are often stalled due to resistance from local communities. Wind power has proven to be controversial, triggering disputes and opposition among groups living near proposed projects (Krekel and Zerrahn, 2017). These groups may be negatively disposed due to auditory or visual distur- bances (Carley et al., 2020), and concerned about impacts on b

At the same time, negative attitudes toward wind power have been shown to elicit value-related emotions, associated with perceptions of distributional or procedural injustice (Wüstenhagen et al., 2007; Knauf, 2022), ideolog

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: e (Wüstenhagen et al., 2007; Knauf, 2022), ideological leanings and trust (Clulow et al., 2021; Vuichard et al., 2019), or environmental concerns (Azarova et al., 2019). title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: 22). Due to high costs, risks, and lengthy construction pro- cesses, nuclear energy has been in decline for several years, yet it could become increasingly relevant with the development of new technolo- gies, such as small modular reactors (IAE, 2023). At COP28 in Dubai, 22 countries signed a pledge to triple nuclear energy capacity by 2050, recognizing the role of nuclear power in keeping the 1.5

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: izing the role of nuclear power in keeping the 1.5 ◦C goal alive (NEA, 2023). The use of nuclear power is, however, disputed due to the risk of accidents and unresolved waste management issues (Gupta et al., 2019; Wang and Kim, 2018). Interestingly though, attitudes toward nuclear power appear to be determined more by political and ideolog- ical factors, values, and worldviews than by risk percept

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ctors, values, and worldviews than by risk perceptions and eco- nomic considerations (Whitfield et al., 2009). Overall, the support for further nuclear investments in the European Union is fairly weak (Eurobarometer, 2022). To successfully navigate a pathway toward a low-carbon and sus- tainable future, it is essential to understand how different social and value-based dimensions influence energy

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ocial and value-based dimensions influence energy policies. This article aims to contribute to the literature by exploring factors that determine energy opinions, focusing on the polarization of wind and nuclear preferences in Sweden. Sweden is an interesting case study, as it is a country with a high level of both wind and nuclear energy in its energy mix, yet one where energy policies are marked

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ergy mix, yet one where energy policies are marked by deep political tensions and polar- ization. The study draws conclusions from a large-scale survey con- ducted in Sweden during 2023, including over 5200 respondents, who were randomly selected and representative of the wider Swedish population. The results show that low-carbon energy investments in Sweden are likely to encounter resistance due

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: in Sweden are likely to encounter resistance due to a sizable antagonistic minority who are strongly opposed to either wind or nuclear energy. Interestingly, among those with traditional, nationalistic, and authoritarian values and right leaning political ideology, the enthusiasm for nuclear energy seems to reduce the closer a new nuclear power plant would be to their own residences. The study hi

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ant would be to their own residences. The study highlights the importance of recognizing the sociopsychological dimensions within political frameworks aiming for a transition toward a low-carbon energy system. 2. Overview of relevant literature Attitudes to wind and nuclear power are divisive in many countries. Both of these energy technologies generate different social emotions in different socie

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: erate different social emotions in different societal groups, often related to beliefs about benefits and risks associated with the respective technology. Factors influencing attitudes to different energy options tend to be contextual and they vary across different countries (Arndt, 2023; Wang and Kim, 2018; Nguyen and Yim, 2018). Opinions about nuclear energy are particularly influenced by normat

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: clear energy are particularly influenced by normative aspects related to the risk of accidents, uranium extraction, the treatment of radioactive waste, and its association with the military industry (Siegrist and Visschers, 2013; Keller et al., 2012; Groh and Mo¨llendorff, 2020; Siegrist et al., 2014). Support for nuclear energy has clearly been affected by the three major accidents worldwide—Thre

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: fected by the three major accidents worldwide—Three Mile Island, Chernobyl, and Fukushima—after which public attitudes turned more negative (Richard Eiser et al., 1990; Visschers and Siegrist, 2013). Nuclear power can, on the other hand, also be associated with energy security and economic prosperity (Slovic et al., 2000; Arndt, 2023), and social acceptance can, thus, be described as a trade-off b

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: cceptance can, thus, be described as a trade-off be- tween risks and potential economic benefits (Finucane et al., 2000; Slovic et al., 2004). People’s perceptions of risks and benefits differ, however, depending on individual-level factors. Aspects that have been demonstrated to in- fluence opinions are political ideology, gender, income, and education (Costa-Font et al., 2008; Sundstro¨m and McC

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ation (Costa-Font et al., 2008; Sundstro¨m and McCright, 2016; Gupta et al., 2019; Spence et al., 2010; Hazboun and Boudet, 2020), as well as institutional and country-level factors, such as the national energy mix and political aspects (Wang and Kim, 2018; Bohdanowicz et al., 2023). 2 E n e r g y P o l i c y 198 (2025) 114492 Renewable energy is not associated with similar risk perceptions, and i

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ot associated with similar risk perceptions, and in many countries, wind energy is among the most popular energy options (Arndt, 2023). Attitudes expressed at nation-level tend to diverge, however, from the views of the communities living in the proximity of wind power investment (Krekel and Zerrahn, 2017; Dug- stad et al., 2020). Bell et al. (2005)expressed accordingly that a “social gap” tends t

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: )expressed accordingly that a “social gap” tends to arise between the high level of public support at the na- tional level, and the low level of successful implementation of wind energy projects. The distance of wind energy sitings from peoples’ residence, the so- called proximity effect, has shown to have a strong impact on opinions (Carley et al., 2020; Karasmanaki and Tsantopoulos, 2021). Early

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: , 2020; Karasmanaki and Tsantopoulos, 2021). Early studies on social acceptance of wind power focused accordingly on the proximity effect or so-called NIMBY-ism (not-in-my-back-yard) (Batel et al., 2013; Fast, 2013). However, there is criticism of the “NIMBY-- term”, which is sometimes used as a method to discredit opponents (Horst, 2007). While noise and visual intrusion of wind farms have shown

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ise and visual intrusion of wind farms have shown to influence public attitudes (Carley et al., 2020; Krekel and Zerrahn, 2017), opposing responses are often based on more complex aspects (Dermont et al., 2017; Batel, 2020). Devine-Wright (2009), Horst (2007) and Dugstad et al. (2023) show that socio-cultural attachment to the place of living is of relevance, and that opposition can be seen as “pl

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: relevance, and that opposition can be seen as “place-protective action”. Public acceptance of wind power is often influenced by the perceived environmental impacts of the wind farm location, and to what extent biodiversity in landscapes are affected (Dugstad et al., 2020). Biodiversity concerns differ, however, between different communities, and in Sweden, they are of particular relevance for Sam

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: n Sweden, they are of particular relevance for Sami communities (Ramasar et al., 2022; Ek and Matti, 2015). Hence, careful analysis of the spatial allocation of wind farms is important when addressing inherent trade-offs between local dis- amenities, biodiversity conservation, and climate change considerations (Kati et al., 2021; Grimsrud et al., 2024). Geographic proximity does not have similar i

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: 024). Geographic proximity does not have similar impacts on attitudes on nuclear energy, and studies show instead that people living close to nuclear power plants are more positively inclined than those living further away (Greenberg, 2009; Uji et al., 2021; Venables et al., 2012; Cale and Kromer, 2015). However, few studies have captured attitudes in relation to proximity prior to the constructio

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: in relation to proximity prior to the construction process. It is likely that new nuclear power plant establishments in landscapes, just like new wind-power plants, may evoke negative reactions motived by sense of place or concerns about their impacts on local wild life and biodiversity (Sovacool, 2012). Recent studies indicate, moreover, that opposition to wind power is associated with notions o

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: osition to wind power is associated with notions of being neglected or misrepresented (Walker and Devine-Wright, 2008). There are also research suggesting that perceptions of unfair distribution of the benefits and burdens of wind power influence acceptance (Huijts et al., 2012; De Luca et al., 2020). If profits from wind power generation is not redistributed locally, but transferred to the nation

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: distributed locally, but transferred to the national level or to business corporations, individuals tend to see the construction of wind power as a kind of exploitation of the local landscape (Lindvall, 2023; Liebe et al., 2017; Knauf, 2022). The decision-making process and the ability of affected individuals to participate in it, also impact acceptance of wind power (Walker and Baxter, 2017; Bren

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: tance of wind power (Walker and Baxter, 2017; Brennan and Rensburg, 2016; Le Maitre et al., 2023; Vuichard et al., 2022). Individuals who distrust public institutions, or who perceive the decision-making processes as unfair, non-transparent, or illegitimate, are also more likely to oppose wind farms (Gross, 2007; Liebe et al., 2017). Perceptions of procedural and distributional justice have also b

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: procedural and distributional justice have also been shown to influence public attitudes to nuclear energy (Visschers and Siegrist, 2012; Besley, 2012), although the research on this matter is limited. Attitudes to different energy options are, however, not only deter- mined by aspects related to fairness and risks, but also by other types of personal norms, values, and ideological orientations.

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: onal norms, values, and ideological orientations. A particularly strong determinant for both wind and nuclear energy attitudes is envi- ronmental concern. Individuals who prioritize environmental protection 2 D. Lindvall et al. over economic aspects, are found to be more skeptical about nuclear energy, while those who perceive nuclear energy as economically beneficial are more prone to downplay en

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: nomically beneficial are more prone to downplay environmental hazards (Pampel, 2011; Badora et al., 2021; Besley and Oh, 2014; Corner et al., 2011). Individuals with pro-environmental attitudes tend to, likewise, be more positive to wind energy (Azarova et al., 2019; Knauf, 2022), although concerns of the local environmental impacts may also motivate resis- tance (Dugstad et al., 2020). Some studi

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: te resis- tance (Dugstad et al., 2020). Some studies indicate that climate change concern has become less relevant for nuclear power acceptance (Bohdanowicz et al., 2023), yet other studies have not found evidence of this trend (Sonnberger et al., 2021; Arndt, 2023). Several studies suggest, moreover, that attitudes toward nuclear energy are associated with political ideology. Individuals who defi

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ated with political ideology. Individuals who define themselves politically to the left tend to be more positive to wind power (Karlstrøm and Ryghaug 2014; Clulow et al., 2021), while the opposite is true for nuclear energy (Whitfield et al., 2009; Costa-Font et al., 2008; Pampel, 2011; Clulow et al., 2021). Worldviews are also relevant, and individuals who express individualistic or hierarchical

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: duals who express individualistic or hierarchical values tend to be more supportive of nuclear energy (Van De Graaff, 2016; Whitfield et al., 2009), while wind power is preferred by individuals who express egalitarian and ecological values (Bidwell, 2013). As a result, attitudes to wind and nuclear energy are prone to po- litical polarization (Perlaviciute and Steg, 2015; Roddis et al., 2019). The

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: aviciute and Steg, 2015; Roddis et al., 2019). The association between ideology and values, and public attitudes to different energy technologies, can therefore be influenced by political narratives or political cues (Latr´e et al., 2019), and media messaging and ˙ political debates (Ho and Kristiansen, 2019; Zuk, 2023; Kojo et al., 2020). Some studies have also shown that attitudes can be exploit

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: dies have also shown that attitudes can be exploited by economic agents or interest groups advocating for or against the usage of renewable energy (Johnstone et al., 2017). 3. Energy policies in Sweden 3.1. The polarised debate on nuclear energy Sweden is a country where energy policies have been fraught with difficult political disputes for over five decades. In the 1970s, Sweden developed a nucl

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ive decades. In the 1970s, Sweden developed a nuclear energy program, commissioning 12 nuclear re- actors. These reactors were constructed between 1972 and 1980, mak- ing Sweden the country with the highest percentage of nuclear power usage per capita in the world (Kåberger, 2002). The construction of nuclear power plants was partly motivated by the needs and demands of the industry, but it was al

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: e needs and demands of the industry, but it was also a response to the 1973 oil price shock and the decision to halt the expansion of hydropower to protect the few remaining unexploited rivers in Sweden (Vedung, 2002). Initially, there was strong interparty consensus for the nuclear energy program; how- ever, the expansion became an increasingly contentious political issue, partly spurred by the a

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ntentious political issue, partly spurred by the anti-nuclear stance taken by the Centre Party (Vedung, 1979). From 1976, nuclear energy emerged as one of the most disruptive issues in Swedish politics, causing a government crisis in 1978 and profoundly affecting the 1979 election results (Holmberg, 2022). Following the accident at Three Mile Island, anti-nuclear senti- ments grew stronger, and to

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: d, anti-nuclear senti- ments grew stronger, and to defuse the issue, a public referendum was held in 1980. A slight majority of voters supported a gradual phase-out of nuclear power in the referendum; however, the process of phasing out nuclear energy did not begin until 1999, when the first reactor was decommissioned. Since then, another five reactors have been closed down (Holmberg, 2009). At th

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: tors have been closed down (Holmberg, 2009). At the time of the referendum, opinions on nuclear energy were not initially influenced by ideological orientations but were determined by factors such as risk perception and environmental concern (Holmberg, 2018). Over time, the issue became increasingly politically polarized, and by 2021, right-leaning voters were, according to one survey, 58 percenta

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: voters were, according to one survey, 58 percentage points more likely to favor nuclear power than left-leaning voters (Holmberg, 2022). In an attempt to depoliticize energy policies, an interparty energy agreement was negotiated by all major political 3 E n e r g y P o l i c y 198 (2025) 114492 parties in 2016. According to this agreement, the existing reactors were allowed to be replaced, but w

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: isting reactors were allowed to be replaced, but without any public subsidies. A long-term target of 100 percent renewable electricity production by 2040 was also adopted, and the so-called electricity certification system, benefiting renewable energy production, was prolonged and expanded (prop: 2017/18:228). In 2019, the agreement was dissolved following the departure of two conservative parties

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ollowing the departure of two conservative parties, which advocated for the continued operation of two reactors at the Ringhals power plant. The debate on nuclear energy then intensified, and in the 2022 election campaign, the pro-nuclear conservative parties ran on a proposal to construct new reactors, while the center-liberal, green, and left-leaning parties took neutral or skeptical stances (Ho

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ning parties took neutral or skeptical stances (Holmberg, 2022). The debate was also influenced by the rising popularity of the Sweden Democrats, a nationalist and right-wing populist party, which strongly advocated for nuclear power and opposed wind power development. The conservative government that took power in 2022, with collaborative support from the Sweden Democrats, declared its intention

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: from the Sweden Democrats, declared its intention to restart one of the decommissioned reactors and grant financial subsidies to any private companies willing to construct new power plants. In 2023, the govern- ment accordingly appointed an inquiry to investigate the construction of new nuclear power plants in Sweden (Regeringen, 2023a). By the end of 2023, the government also presented its Climat

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: of 2023, the government also presented its Climate Action Plan, in compliance with the Climate Act, in which it claimed that “… in Sweden nuclear power is the single most important measure to reduce our emission nationally …” (Regeringen, 2023b, p. 10). 3.2. The divided support for wind power The expansion of wind power has also become an issue characterized by growing political tension. Wind pow

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: aracterized by growing political tension. Wind power has developed rapidly in Sweden, and in between 2001 and 2021, the installed effect increased by almost ten times (Swedish Energy Agency, 2021). The expansion of wind power has been widely supported in society, yet in recent years, atti- tudes has become increasingly polarized (Jo¨nsson, 2022). Just like the debate on nuclear power, political op

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ust like the debate on nuclear power, political opinions are divided between conservative parties, which express anti-wind power rhetoric, and the more supportive center-liberal, green, and left-leaning parties (Axelsson et al., 2023). At the local level, wind power has become a particularly divisive issue. The Environmental Act and the municipal planning monopoly grant municipalities the power to

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: lanning monopoly grant municipalities the power to decline con- struction permits for wind power sites, colloquially referred to as the “municipal veto.” The number of municipal rejections has increased in recent years, and in 2021, around 80 percent of all construction appli- cations were declined (Westander, 2022). A key reason for these re- jections is the lack of formal compensation for people

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ions is the lack of formal compensation for people living near wind power sites (Lindvall, 2023). As a result, the government commissioned a public inquiry in 2021 to present proposals for a more predictable municipal siting process (SOU, 2022:53), and in 2022, another inquiry was appointed to introduce local incentives for wind power develop- ment (SOU, 2023:18). The new conservative government,

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: (SOU, 2023:18). The new conservative government, elected in 2022, expressed a negative stance on wind power during the election campaign and has withdrawn public subsidies for the connection cost of offshore wind power to the power grid. In its Climate Action Plan, the government eased its negative policy stance towards wind power, although it did not present any proposals for facilitating wind p

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: not present any proposals for facilitating wind power development or overcoming acceptance barriers (Regeringen, 2023b). 4. Research hypotheses, methodology and data material 4.1. Hypothesizing determinants for acceptance of energy investment The aim of this article is to examine potential determinants of social attitudes to wind and nuclear energy, based on the results of an exploratory survey.

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: y, based on the results of an exploratory survey. Previous studies have demonstrated that environ- mental concern is a key determinant for both wind and nuclear power, but also that political attitudes and values, such as ideology, 3 D. Lindvall et al. worldviews, and personal identity, influence attitudes to these energy options (Karlstrøm and Ryghaug 2014; Clulow et al., 2021; Pampel, 2011; Whit

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: haug 2014; Clulow et al., 2021; Pampel, 2011; Whitfield et al., 2009). Based on these previous findings, we as- sume that individuals with right-leaning attitudes and low climate concern, express higher support to nuclear energy, while left-leaning individuals with high climate concern, express higher support for wind power. We also assume that worldviews, that correspond with traditional, authori

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ldviews, that correspond with traditional, authoritarian, and nationalistic (TAN), or green, alternative, and libertarian (GAL) values (Hooghe et al., 2002), predict energy preferences, as well as political and institutional trust. To explore these correlations, we will test the following hypothesis. H1. Value -based orientations influence peoples’ propensity to support nuclear and wind power. In

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: propensity to support nuclear and wind power. In a situation with a high level of political polarization between different energy policies, it is likely that energy preferences are deter- mined by the attitudes toward one technology. Such effects can indicate that attitudes are determined by political cues (Latr´e et al., 2019) or politically motivated reasoning (Kahan, 2013), rather than environ

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: vated reasoning (Kahan, 2013), rather than environ- mental concerns, risk perceptions, economic benefits, energy security or other factual considerations. There could, thus, be a correlation between support for nuclear power and negative attitudes to wind power, while the correlation is reversed for support for wind power. Such a rela- tionship could indicate that polarization is a factor that can

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ld indicate that polarization is a factor that can explain at- titudes. To explore this, we will test the following hypothesis. H2. The propensity to support nuclear power is determined by nega- tive attitudes toward wind power. Another factor that has been shown to strongly influence support for wind power is the location of the sitings (Batel et al., 2013; Carley et al., 2020). The closer a wind

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: al., 2013; Carley et al., 2020). The closer a wind farm is constructed to where people live, the less likely they are to accept it. This proximity effect has not previously been shown to strongly affect people’s attitudes to nuclear power, yet it is uncertain whether people hold supportive attitudes prior to the con- struction process. It is, moreover, not clear how value-based orientations intera

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ver, not clear how value-based orientations interact with, or alleviate, the proximity effect, which could indicate how strongly attitudes are underpinned by cultural values. To explore these associations, we test the following three hypotheses. H3. The proximity of a wind power project predicts attitudes toward wind power development. H4. The proximity of a nuclear power project predicts attitude

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: imity of a nuclear power project predicts attitudes toward nuclear power development. H5. Value-based orientations mediate the proximity effect on attitudes toward nuclear and wind power. 4.2. Survey design and data The study is based on data from a large-scale public opinion survey on attitudes to climate and energy policies in Sweden. The survey was conducted within the research project Fairtran

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: was conducted within the research project Fairtrans by the survey company Skop. It included questions on both climate and energy policies, and a subset of the questions was analyzed for this article. The sample was drawn from the population register and made as a non-proportional stratified sample in three age categories and divisions between different types of municipality, in order to attain a p

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: rent types of municipality, in order to attain a proportional repre- sentation. It was sent out in postal format, with possibilities for digital responses, to 33 432 respondents aged 18 to 84 in between 25 April and 23 August, 2023. A post-stratification methodology was applied to make the sample representative of the total population. After weighting the dataset on a number of core demographics t

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ing the dataset on a number of core demographics to make it representative of the Swedish population, a total of 5280 respondents were included in the dataset. More information about the data collection and stratifica- tion methodology is provided in the supplementary material to this article. To capture both general attitudes and proximity effects, participants 4 E n e r g y P o l i c y 198 (2025

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: , participants 4 E n e r g y P o l i c y 198 (2025) 114492 were asked to respond to three statements regarding each energy tech- nology. The statements were phrased; To speed up the transformation to a fossil free society, I support further construction of wind/nuclear power in Sweden. Thereafter, the respondents were asked if they support (1) construction of wind/nuclear power in their municipali

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: truction of wind/nuclear power in their municipality and (2) construction of wind/nuclear power within 5 km from their home. Five kilometers was estimated to be considered as intrusive, realistic, and comparable between the two energy options. For each statement, the respondents were asked to indicate their level of agreement on a seven- point scale. The scale was anchored at 1 = Completely disagr

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: e. The scale was anchored at 1 = Completely disagree,1 4 = Neither/nor, and 7 =Completely agree. We thereafter designed questions on value-based orientations, aspiring to both capture positions on the left/right political spectrum and social ideals and worldviews. On the later issue, we used a scale inspired by the GAL (Green/Alternative/Libertarian) vs. TAN (Tradi- tional/Authoritarian/Nationalis

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: n) vs. TAN (Tradi- tional/Authoritarian/Nationalistic) framework (Hooghe et al., 2002). GAL/TAN values are typically assessed using a set of value judgments regarding aspects such as multiculturalism, international solidarity, gender equality on one end, and conservatism and national community on the other (Hobolt et al., 2021). GAL values were assessed on the re- sponses to the following two ques

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: essed on the re- sponses to the following two questions: “Society is enriched by open bor- ders, global cooperation and international solidarity” and “It is important to promote equality between men and women”. TAN was assessed on the following two questions: “In a secure society, immigration is low and the majority of the inhabitants share the same culture” and “In a secure society, law and order

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: e culture” and “In a secure society, law and order is maintained by a strong police force and a judiciary that can sentence cognizable punishments for criminals”. GAL is the weighted re- sponses of the two first questions, and the opposite for TAN. Two questions aimed to measure trust were created, asking re- spondents to assess if they believed politicians to most often act in the public interest

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: liticians to most often act in the public interests (political trust) and if public authorities (governmental trust) most often act competently, efficiently and in accordance with the rule of law. The same response scale as described above was used. To capture environmental concern, a subset of Schultz’s scale (Wesley schultz, 2001) was used. Respondents were asked to indicate their level of conce

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ndents were asked to indicate their level of concern about environmental problems and their conse- quences to (1) myself, (2) all people, (3) my lifestyle, (4) people close to me, (5) animals and plants, (6) future generations, and (7) children, on a seven-point scale. The scale was anchored at 1 =Not at all concerned and 7 =Very concerned. The responses were collated into an index assessing envir

text: ponses were collated into an index assessing environmental concern. Other individual characteristics gathered in the survey regarded in- come, education, place of living (rural/urban), and gender. Discussion Our study reveals that individual values notably forecast attitudes towards both wind or nuclear power, aligning with our initial hypoth- esis. Consistent with prior studies, it is evident that environmental concern significantly shapes the views on wind power (Arndt, 2023; Azarova et al.

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: e views on wind power (Arndt, 2023; Azarova et al., 2019; Perlaviciute and Steg, 2015), and resistance to nuclear power (Whitfield et al., 2009; de Groot et al., 2013). Wind power can impact on biodiversity and the local environment (Adeyeye et al., 2020), still in our study environmental concern was strongly associated with wind power support, even though we captured environmental at- titudes wit

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: n though we captured environmental at- titudes with the usage of a broad measurement, not only related to for climate change. It can be assumed that a great number of people asso- ciate nuclear energy with ecological and human risks, as suggested by Whitfield et al. (2009), and this can partly explain why environmentally concerned individuals are skeptical to nuclear energy. The assumption that nu

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: keptical to nuclear energy. The assumption that nuclear energy has become popular as a low-carbon alterative in the energy transition, as suggested by Bohdanowicz et al. (2023), is not supported by this study. To overcome this type of resistance, policy- makers may consider awareness campaigns, addressing concerns regarding safety of nuclear energy, while emphasizing its potential environmental be

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: , while emphasizing its potential environmental benefits. Lindvall et al. The strongest determinants for support or resistance to wind and nuclear energy are, however, worldviews and

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: d and nuclear energy are, however, worldviews and ideological orientations. Nuclear energy is strongly correlated with right leaning political orientation, while individuals with left leaning political views, are more supportive of wind power. It is unclear if the respondents had full un- derstanding of the economic costs or benefits of the two energy options. Personal beliefs and values may, on t

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: rgy options. Personal beliefs and values may, on the other hand, influence public receptiveness of information about different energy alternatives. Attitudes are also likely to be subject to political polarization. A rather remarkable finding in this regard was the exceptionally high correlation between social ideals and cultural values and different en- ergy options. Individuals who embrace openn

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: nt en- ergy options. Individuals who embrace openness, multiethnic tolerance and gender equality prefer wind power, while those expressing authoritarian and patriotic worldviews, favor nuclear energy. It can thus be concluded that energy politics in Sweden is divided along the so- called GAL/TAN scale (Hooghe et al., 2002). Interactions between opinions about energy preferences and world- views, e

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: nions about energy preferences and world- views, e.g. individualistic, egalitarian or hierarchical worldviews, have been found in previous studies (Van De Graaff, 2016; Perlaviciute and Steg, 2015). Explaining these associations, Groh and Mo¨llendorff (2020) claim that proponents of nuclear energy tend to take human dominance over nature for granted, and therefore have less anxiety of the human an

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: d, and therefore have less anxiety of the human and ecological consequences of potential accidents. Upon this assump- tion, it is also reasonable to believe that individuals who are not endorsing principles of gender equality nor displaying strong solidarity with other people, take a more dominant position in society and in relation to nature, in accordance with the theory of Social Dominance Orie

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ccordance with the theory of Social Dominance Orientation (Pratto et al., 1994). Previous research has shown that groups that express group-based dominance tend to justify or promote social and racial inequalities, hierarchies and discrimination (Jost and Thompson, 2000), while there are also correlations between social dominance and ignorance of environmental degradation (Milfont and Sibley, 2014

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: nvironmental degradation (Milfont and Sibley, 2014) and climate denial (Jylha¨ and Akrami, 2015). High TAN and low GAL scores may thus reflect a similar predispo- sition towards social dominance orientation. Notably, individuals with such orientations exhibited heightened endorsement for nuclear energy and resistance towards wind power. Consequently, it can be inferred that values aligning with so

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: y, it can be inferred that values aligning with social hierarchy correlate with a belief in human dominance over nature. This inclination may lead individuals to downplay the potential risks associated with nuclear energy and over- emphasize negative aspects of wind power. In an inverted manner, in- dividuals who display solidarity for other people and advocate for gender equality, are instead lik

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: and advocate for gender equality, are instead likely to incorporate concerns for the environment, and thus being more skeptical to nuclear power and more supportive for wind energy (Whitfield et al., 2009). Another plausible interpretation of the correlations between value expressions and energy opinions could be attributed to the political polarization inherent in Swedish energy politics. It is

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: zation inherent in Swedish energy politics. It is probable that in- dividuals whose political affiliations and cultural values align with the extreme ends on the ideological spectrum are swayed by political de- bates, and that they adjust their opinions accordingly. Political parties have shown to have substantial influence over public opinion formation, as voters are often guided by political cue

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ation, as voters are often guided by political cues (Goren et al., 2009), and this may explain the polarization of energy preferences. With increasing ideological polarization, political views can more- over be transformed into identities, shaping our wider interpretations of reality and becoming aspects motivating our reasoning about various political objects (Cohen, 2003), including energy techn

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ical objects (Cohen, 2003), including energy technologies. The psychological urge of belonging to a social group can in this sense contribute to furnish our worldviews, making us unconsciously conform our views in line with the opinions expressed by communities that share our identity and political affiliation. Facts that are in conflict with group membership can therefore trigger a form of uncons

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: membership can therefore trigger a form of unconscious identity self-defense, which motivate individuals to resist empirical facts (Kahan, 2013). There is robust evidence for the influence of motivated reasoning for climate policy formation (Druckman and McGrath, 2019), and it can, 8 E n e r g y P o l i c y 198 (2025) 114492 thus, be expected that the same phenomena emerge in energy politics, par

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: the same phenomena emerge in energy politics, particularly in Sweden where the debate has been characterized by deep polarization. To measure trends of group polarization and actual prev- alence of motivated reasoning, we would need longitudinal data to capture changes of attitudes over time, and for this reason we cannot draw any firm conclusions on these matters. We did find support, however, f

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: on these matters. We did find support, however, for our second hypothesis, noticing that negative wind power attitudes determine positive attitudes to nuclear power, and vice versa. Another remarkable finding was the strong correlation between governmental trust and energy opinions. High trusting individuals are supportive of wind power, while individuals with low trust prefer nu- clear power. At

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ividuals with low trust prefer nu- clear power. At face value this correlation appears contradictory, given that it can be presumed that people who lack trust in public institutions would not support an energy technology that require expert involve- ment and an institutional infrastructure to operate safely. This result also contradicts previous findings regarding associations between trust and nu

Introduction The German energy transition (Energiewende) has long been a role model for other countries (

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ) has long been a role model for other countries (Strunz, 2014). In 2021, Germany led 11% of the newly installed onshore and offshore wind installations in Europe, ranking as the third country after the United Kingdom and Sweden (WindEurope, 2022). Germany is expected to install the most wind ca- pacity in Europe by 2026. Nonetheless, in 2020, the lowest installations and investments were seen sin

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: lowest installations and investments were seen since 2010. The COVID-19 pandemic can partly explain this, but also by complex and lengthy permitting pro- cesses and the challenges connected to the switch to an auction scheme in 2017 (Lundberg, 2019). Recent changes to this legislation will allow civic wind energy projects to be exempt from the auction system in 2023.1 However, the German wind indu

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ion system in 2023.1 However, the German wind industry is currently facing not only challenges related to permitting, legislation, and auctions but also social barriers (Kimm, 2017). The transition to renewable energy sources is affected by dynamics that go beyond technical issues, being part of a political

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: beyond technical issues, being part of a political, social, cultural, and spatial transformation. To facilitate a successful transition from fossil fuels to renewables next to the strategic site planning of federal, state and/or local governments, it is important to obtain the acceptance of local communities (Huijts et al., 2012; Warren et al., 2005). To enable a socially just energy transition, a

text: 5). To enable a socially just energy transition, a better understanding of the concerns and motivations of local communities is inevitable. These could generate valuable insights into how projects should be sited, designed, commu- nicated, and implemented (Olson-Hazboun et al., 2016). Understanding why some projects face resistance while others are supported is neces- sary (Christidis et al., 2017

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: supported is neces- sary (Christidis et al., 2017; Wolsink, 2007). Various studies have investigated different factors that influence local responses to wind en- ergy projects; however, the role of local political figures has received little attention so far and is thus a promising area of research. This paper aims to investigate the dynamics of local responses to four wind energy (N. Schneider).

Karakislak and N. Schneider projects in Bavaria, Germany, focusing on the role of local politicians and social norms. German federal states define guidelines that regulate wind

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ederal states define guidelines that regulate wind energy siting. Therefore, we chose only one federal state (Bavaria) to ensure that the regulatory framework was the same. One common regulatory path for addressing acceptance-related problems is determining re- quirements for their proximity to residential areas (Masurowski et al., 2016; Watson et al., 2012). Bavaria has introduced a state regulat

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: al., 2012). Bavaria has introduced a state regulation, the 10H rule, which defines the minimum distance between the wind turbine and residential areas as at least ten times the total height of the wind turbine (Baugesetzbuch, 2014). With wind turbines reaching overall heights of 250 m, the 10H rule increases the challenge for mu- nicipalities to find appropriate project sites. Even though proximit

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: nd appropriate project sites. Even though proximity has some effect on the perceptions of wind farms, the intensity of this effect is shaped by the norms and values of the affected communities (van der Horst, 2007). Political power can produce and promote certain norms in social systems (Fraser, 2014), and institutional norms have the potential to regulate market dynamics (Nyborg et al., 2016). Fu

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: regulate market dynamics (Nyborg et al., 2016). Further, this paper focuses on wind energy projects in forest areas because some of the main concerns of those who oppose wind energy projects are related to landscape protection and impacts on biodiversity, especially in forest areas (Dai et al., 2015). While deploying wind parks in forest areas is complex in terms of local acceptance in Germany, de

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: omplex in terms of local acceptance in Germany, developing wind energy in such zones is necessary for achieving climate and renewable energy goals (FA Wind, 2021). Wind turbines in forested areas are situated almost exclusively in the southern federal states, namely, Rhineland-Palatinate, Hessen, Bavaria, Baden-Württemberg, and North Rhine-Westphalia (Bunzel et al., 2019). In Bavaria, forests acco

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ia (Bunzel et al., 2019). In Bavaria, forests account for around 37% of the land area, making it the state with the greatest forest coverage among all 16 German states (FA Wind, 2021). Analysing local actors that influence community norms can increase understanding of conflicts and dynamics related to wind energy projects (Karakislak et al., 2021). This paper aims to fill the gaps in the literatur

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: This paper aims to fill the gaps in the literature by exploring the relationship between local political figures and social norms and examining their effect on local responses to wind energy projects. It does so by addressing the following research questions: 1) What is the role of elected mayors in wind energy projects? 2) What influence do their opinions have on local responses? and 3) What act

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: opinions have on local responses? and 3) What actors and processes influence local responses over time? The remainder of this article is structured as follows: Section 2 in- troduces the theoretical foundation of the paper by discussing the role of social norms, local political figures, and the local population. Section 3 introduces the methodological approach, the case study selection, the metho

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ical approach, the case study selection, the methods, and the analytical approach. Section 4 gives a detailed over- view of the case studies. Section 5 illustrates the results of the four case studies. The paper concludes in section 6 by reviewing the main findings and suggesting implications for policymakers and practitioners. 2. text: itical agency and local responses This paper applies the conceptual framework described by Kar- akislak et al. (2021). This two-dimensional framework specifies factors and variables for analysing the definitions, influencing factors, and impacts of social norms as well as perceived justice within communities. The first element of the framework proposes that three groups of in- dividuals are influe

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: oses that three groups of in- dividuals are influential in terms of the norms that impact local re- sponses: community spokespersons, political figures, and opposition groups. These actor groups come to power to influence public opinion in situations where there is uncertainty or conflicts, such as unequal dis- tribution of benefits. Community spokespersons have social influence and strong ties wi

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: espersons have social influence and strong ties within their groups, individuals with resources and networks capable of impacting others (Karakislak et al., 2021). Their opinion (e.g., support, oppose) on energy projects does not define their role, but their impact on the community and processes matters, whereas opposition groups foster a negative norm. Mayors and local council members are actors

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: norm. Mayors and local council members are actors with political power, social resources, and strong 2 E n e r g y P o l i c y 1 76(2023)113509 ties to the community. Therefore, they have a key influence on local responses to wind energy projects (Karakislak et al., 2021). Social norms How members of the public respond to the social and environmental changes around them is widely linked to the expected reactions of others. These conditional expectations about how people will react, or how they should react, are conditional beh

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: act, or how they should react, are conditional behavioural regularities - or so- cial norms (Bicchieri and Mercier, 2014). Overcoming conflicts associated with energy projects requires a deeper understanding of the embedded influences and values related to the social context (Upham and Johansen, 2020). There tends to be a gap between what others typically do (descriptive norms) and what ought to b

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: pically do (descriptive norms) and what ought to be done (injunctive norms) (White et al., 2009). The distinction between intention and behaviour is also relevant when estimating responses to wind energy projects (Sokoloski et al., 2018). Social influence2 within groups tends to increase the effects of normative beliefs about renewable energy technologies in general (Hübner et al., 2023; Reyes-Mer

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ologies in general (Hübner et al., 2023; Reyes-Mercado and Rajagopal, 2017). Moreover, social norms or pressure from family, friends, and neighbours, alongside political actors, have the potential to influence local responses in both directions (Huijts et al., 2012). This study addresses local responses to projects as implicit normative indicators of communities. Descriptive norms about climate ch

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: of communities. Descriptive norms about climate change mitigation are also factors that are considered. 2.2. The role of local political figures Local politicians are directly affected by public opinion, while in turn their engagement in wind energy projects affects local responses (Friedl and Reichl, 2016). Consequently, local politicians may have a significant impact by shaping the dynamics of c

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: a significant impact by shaping the dynamics of community acceptance of wind en- ergy projects. Active local support for community-led projects3 strongly shapes the distribution of power and the relationships between local actors (Bell et al., 2013). As key agents of societal inclusion and local policies, the needs and views of the mayors should be better understood (Gürtler and Herberg, 2021). Y

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: e better understood (Gürtler and Herberg, 2021). Young and Brans (2017) and Beermann (2009) found from their case studies that the role of the mayor as a policy entrepreneur in implementing 100% renewable energy systems is crucial. In other cul- tural contexts, for example, a case study comparison in Japan underlined the role of shared social norms about community initiatives for renew- able energ

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: about community initiatives for renew- able energy as part of policy learning by mayoral leadership and other stakeholders (Takao, 2020). Mayors may become advocates or leaders of renewable energy projects that influence local citizens (Honva´ri and Kukorelli, 2018). Local politicians have the social and political power to influence public opinion (Busch and McCormick, 2014; Friedl and Reichl, 20

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: (Busch and McCormick, 2014; Friedl and Reichl, 2016; Karakislak et al., 2021). The power of the local politicians may be applied in different ways. Partzsch (2016) explains this using three concepts in environmental politics: “power with”, which includes learning and cooperation; “power to”, which involves resistance and empowerment; and “power over”, which refers to manipulation. These understan

text: er”, which refers to manipulation. These understandings of power are embedded in actors, agents, and structures that influence decisions (Partzsch, 2016).
