title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: articleundertheCCBYlicense(http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/). M. Scovell et al. Siegrist, for example, found that people tend to be more accepting of solar power than other energy technologies such as nuclear, wind and natural gas [6]. Similar results have also been found in Australia, with people tending to support the use of wind and solar over other energy technologies like hydro-pow

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: olar over other energy technologies like hydro-power, nuclear, coal and gas [5]. Another study comparing levels of renewable energy acceptance across countries in Europe also found that solar power had the highest level of support of all renewable energy technologies [8]. Overall, these findings suggest that people, for the most part, like the idea of using more solar power in the future; though i

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: of using more solar power in the future; though it is less clear on people’s perceptions of large scale solar farms. Furthermore, high levels of broader public acceptance does not necessarily translate into high levels of acceptance at the smaller com- munity level [9–13]. Some community members experience, or expect to experience, more of the costs (e.g., local environmental impacts) and less of

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ts (e.g., local environmental impacts) and less of the benefits (e.g., wealth creation) associated with an energy production technology than the broader population [12]. It is not un- common for publicly supported renewable energy projects to be met with local opposition [14]. Wind farms, for example, are strongly sup- ported at the population level but often face local opposition for a va- riety

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: l but often face local opposition for a va- riety of reasons [15,16]. Few studies have, though, comprehensively investigated how communities think about, and are likely to respond to, large-scale solar farms [2,4,17], especially the role of the wider energy transition narrative on local community acceptance. 1.2. Local community acceptance of renewable energy developments Recent empirical research

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: able energy developments Recent empirical research has investigated levels of local support for utility-scale solar energy installations and the factors that influence it. Carlisle and colleagues in a series of studies found that local support for utility-scale solar in the US is high, but varies depending on a number of factors [2,17,18]. In two studies, they found that local support for utility-

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: tudies, they found that local support for utility-scale solar in California was significantly influenced by perceived positive impacts like jobs and increased property value [2,18]. They also found that, contrary to past research [11], place-based beliefs (e.g., level of agreement that where they lived was beautiful, had a great commu- nity of people etc.) and perceived negative impacts had no sig

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: le etc.) and perceived negative impacts had no significant influence on support for utility-scale solar when controlling for other factors like perceived positive impacts [2,18]. In another study, using a sample from the whole US population, it was found that political ide- ology, believing that a solar farm would spoil scenery, and trust in the developers were significant predictors of acceptance

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: velopers were significant predictors of acceptance of a solar farm in one’s local county [17]. Other factors like the perceived seriousness of climate change and believing that a solar development would decrease property value were not significant predictors of local acceptance [17]. These findings suggest the perceived personal and community benefits of a local solar project are particularly stro

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: its of a local solar project are particularly strong drivers of local acceptance. However, the statistical models in all these studies only explained relatively small amounts of variability (between 18 and 40 % in sample) in local support, which suggests other important factors need to be included to more comprehensively explain solar farm acceptance. More recent research investigating local accep

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ce. More recent research investigating local acceptance suggests that while communities are not against large-scale solar, they are more supportive of smaller scale solar applications [4,19]. Two recent studies, for instance, found that support for large ‘utility-scale’ solar was significantly lower than support for rooftop or community-scale solar [4, 19]. The findings suggest that as the size of

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: [4, 19]. The findings suggest that as the size of the projects increase, the level of support tends to decrease. Cousse also found that, contrary to past findings, people express similar levels of support for solar and wind when controlling for the size of the project suggesting that the type of technology is less important than the overall footprint [4]. One expla- nation for this finding is tha

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: int [4]. One expla- nation for this finding is that larger-scale solar projects seem to be viewed as an ‘industrial activity’ and thus people tend to think more about the local impacts [19]. Theories of social licence and reviews of empirical research highlight a few key factors that help to explain local acceptance of energy-related 2 R e n e w a b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e v i e w s 189(2024)114029 projects [10,20–22]. These beliefs and attitudes can be broadly cat- egorised into three main explanatory factors: (1) beliefs about a project’s consequences (2) social trust and relationships with industry operators, and (3) implicit attitudes and/or feelings about the energy technology. This section will briefly summariz

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: rgy technology. This section will briefly summarize these three main belief/attitude categories and highlight how they are usually operationalised in empirical research. One important set of beliefs that is a reliable predictor of local acceptance is how people think about the consequences (i.e., effects) of an energy project. Perceived consequences are often categorised as the perceived negative

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: s are often categorised as the perceived negative impacts (e.g., perceived risks and/or costs) and the perceived benefits (both local and societal). Studies that measure perceived impacts (or perceived risk/cost) find that the more people agree that a project will cause negative outcomes, the less they support it [23–28]. Conversely, believing that an energy project will bring about benefits is po

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: an energy project will bring about benefits is positively related to acceptance [29–33]. There is some evi- dence to suggest that perceived individual and community effects of a solar farm (both benefits and impacts) are also important for explaining local solar farm acceptance [2,18]. However, the past research investi- gating large scale solar farms uses relatively few survey items to assess pe

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: arms uses relatively few survey items to assess perceived impacts and benefits. As such, it is relatively unclear from previous research what types of perceived effects are important for explaining solar farm acceptance and why. Of particular importance is the extent to which people trust the company involved with an energy project or the development of a technology [20,34]. Several studies have s

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: nt of a technology [20,34]. Several studies have shown that social trust is strongly associated with acceptance in many different contexts [26, 35–37], including the acceptance of solar farms [17]. However, the findings are mixed regarding the extent to which social trust explains acceptance in less contentious energy industries like renewable energy projects [9,23]. Associated with social trust,

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: gy projects [9,23]. Associated with social trust, other psychosocial factors also seem to explain local acceptance of energy projects. For example, procedural fairness, believing that the community has been fairly involved in a company’s decision making, and relationship quality, believing that there is a good community relationship with a company, have both been shown to be strongly association w

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: have both been shown to be strongly association with trust and, in turn, local acceptance [26,35]. In addition to these factors, other research finds that distributive fairness (i.e., believing that local benefits will be shared fairly) and governance (i.e., believing that governance would be effective and trustworthy) also help to explain variability of a measure of local acceptance [26]. While

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: lity of a measure of local acceptance [26]. While these factors do seem to explain local acceptance in contentious energy industries (e.g., mining and coal seam gas extraction) it is unclear from past research how much these addi- tional contextual factors help to explain local solar farm acceptance. Beyond more explicit beliefs about a project’s impacts and the context, affect and/or feelings ten

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: mpacts and the context, affect and/or feelings tend to be another reliable predictor of energy technology acceptance. Affect is a broad term that can encom- pass general moods, specific emotions and feelings.1 Psychological research has consistently shown that people’s affect and/or feelings are strongly associated with explicit attitudes, preferences and behaviour [38,39]. The affect heuristic pr

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ces and behaviour [38,39]. The affect heuristic provides one explanation for this finding and posits that people tag attitude objects (people, places, technology) with either positive or negatively valanced imagery [40,41]. Put simply, people tend to form quick evaluations of things based on feelings of relative ‘goodness’ or ‘badness’ they associate with it. There is some evidence to suggest the

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: te with it. There is some evidence to suggest the feelings people associate with solar farms help to explain their reported level of acceptance. For example, Cousse found that one of the strongest predictors of local concern about a large-scale solar farm was the affective responses people report (i.e., ‘liking’ or ‘disliking’) when shown pictures of a solar farm [4]. The same study found that whi

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: of a solar farm [4]. The same study found that whilst people tended to think of 1 Affect is sometimes uses synonymous with the concept of an attitude. That is, an explicit (or implicit) liking or disliking of an attitude object. The word feeling instead of affect is used in this research to differentiate it from attitude. 2 M. Scovell et al. positively valanced words when asked to think about sola

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: vely valanced words when asked to think about solar panels in general, when respondents were presented with pictures of large-scale solar farms they tended to think of more negatively valanced words. The results suggest people tend to associate images of larger scale pro- jects with more negatively imbued thoughts, which in turn, lowers community acceptance [4]. These findings are supported by oth

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: cceptance [4]. These findings are supported by other research showing that people tend to think about solar power, and other renewables, in an abstract way, which tends to lead to more favourable evaluations [9]. However, when people are asked to think more about the potential negative impacts of a solar farm project (e.g., toxic waste generated during production), then acceptance is lower [9]. Th

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ring production), then acceptance is lower [9]. The find- ings suggests that the feelings people associate with solar farms should help to explain acceptance. 1.3. Wider public attitudes toward the environment and energy While most studies investigating social acceptance of energy tech- nologies tend to focus on contextual factors, other research suggests that broader beliefs people hold about rel

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: uggests that broader beliefs people hold about related topics (e.g., climate change, the environment, politics) help to shape how people think about energy projects [42]. Several studies have, for example, shown that pro- environmental beliefs (and/or environmental concern) and specific values (e.g., altruism) help to shape attitudes towards a range of energy technologies [16,43–46]. Moreover, the

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: e of energy technologies [16,43–46]. Moreover, these values seem to influence acceptance through their influence on more context specific beliefs like the perceived risk and benefit of a technology [16,29,42,47]. There is evidence to suggest that broader beliefs and values also influence local solar farm acceptance. Carlisle et al. highlights that broader beliefs about the environment and one’s po

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: broader beliefs about the environment and one’s political affiliation are related to local support for utility-scale solar [17]. Cultural theory posits that environmental values, beliefs, and attitudes interplay with political perspectives and about how best to address environmental issues such as climate change [48]. This interplay is reflected in broad worldviews about relationships between huma

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: broad worldviews about relationships between humans and nature, the robustness of the environment to change, the need for human intervention, and the role of government and technology [49]. Another important aspect that has received little consideration in past research is the extent to which people support more specific nar- ratives about the renewable energy transition [50,51]. A narrative typi

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ewable energy transition [50,51]. A narrative typically addresses why a technology is needed, why this technology over other options, and how this technology solution fits into a broader solution, in this case as part of a contested energy mix [50]. The narrative reflects the underlying rationale as to how effectively the technology addresses a broader problem or issue. Narratives have been identi

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ader problem or issue. Narratives have been identified as important in shaping public debate and critical in speeding up or slowing down a transition to novel technologies [52]. The narrative around the energy transition developing in Australia has different dimensions based on the role that individual energy sources are seen to play in the economy and how they play into the trilemma of energy nee

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: and how they play into the trilemma of energy needs [53,54]. Australia, for example, is a country that has created much of its wealth due to mineral and energy extraction and export. As such, energy transition narratives include implications for these industry sectors. The energy narrative also stipulates a need for energy to be affordable, sustainable and reliable for the everyday household and

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: nable and reliable for the everyday household and for the functioning of the economy. This implies a po- tential trade-off: the willingness of households to trade-off energy affordability and reliability for a speedier energy transition. 3 R e n e w a b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e v i e w s 189(2024)114029 1.4. A country experiencing an energy transition The public discussion o

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ncing an energy transition The public discussion of the energy mix of the future also needs to be set in context of the role that energy plays in the revenue of the country and national economic benefits. Australia is an energy exporter with coal mining accounting for 1.7 % and activities from oil and gas extraction accounting for around 2 % of the gross domestic product [55] 2. Additionally, roya

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: gross domestic product [55] 2. Additionally, royalties contribute around $10 billion or 4.4 % of the states’ general finance statistics revenue [55].3 Almost 19 % of Aus- tralia’s product exports in 2019 were fuels [56] and the country is one of the top four global exporters of LNG and a top two coal exporter [57]. Like many countries dependent on fossil fuel related revenues, a tran- sition to t

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: fossil fuel related revenues, a tran- sition to the use of renewables and phasing out of fossil fuels extraction can be perceived as a risk for this source of revenue and economic ac- tivity in the country. It is, therefore, likely that public beliefs about the broader energy system in Australia and, particularly its historical reli- ance on the fossil fuel industry for economic growth, will infl

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ossil fuel industry for economic growth, will influence how people think about renewables and, in turn, solar farms. Different energy resources will have different timelines for being phased in or phased out. In global demand scenarios based on stated policies and announced pledges, natural gas still appears to have a long- term role to play in the energy mix of countries and plausibly in Aus- tra

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: energy mix of countries and plausibly in Aus- tralia’s energy exports [58]. Perceptions about natural gas and its future role in Australia [53], as well as the country’s capacity to become an exporter of renewable energy should also influence views about the role for renewables and local acceptance of solar farms. Determining the extent to which such view influence local acceptance also has impli

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: uch view influence local acceptance also has implica- tions for other countries outside of Australia experiencing a similar en- ergy transition who also rely on natural gas as part of their energy system. 1.5. Aims This study has two main aims. The first is to provide a descriptive representation of the social licence factors that are associated with local large-scale solar farm acceptance. Past r

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: th local large-scale solar farm acceptance. Past research highlights the impor- tance of explanatory factors such as perceived impacts, benefits and social trust for explaining local acceptance of energy technologies. However, the relative importance of such factors, using a comprehensive set of measures, for explaining the local acceptance of solar farms has yet to be tested empirically. The seco

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: r farms has yet to be tested empirically. The second aim of this study is to explore the extent to which broader beliefs about the energy transition narrative may further influence local solar farm acceptance (directly and via other explanatory factors). As this study uses an exploratory data analysis approach, no specific predictions are made about the relative impor- tance of specific variables,

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: t the relative impor- tance of specific variables, causal mechanisms, or the validity of a spe- cific theoretical model. Instead, the goal of this study is to identify robust effects in a large, representative sample to aid future research and theory development. The findings are also relevant to practitioners seeking to understand the key factors underpinning social acceptance of large-scale sola

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: underpinning social acceptance of large-scale solar farms and how these are influenced by energy transition narra- tives. The research is of particular relevance to those parts of the world where fossil fuel related industries still hold a key role in the economy. 2. In survey research, the goal is to obtain a representative sample of target population to make reliable inferences about that population. This rese

text: liable inferences about that population. This research used an undirected network modelling approach to determine the conditional associations between a number of theoretically relevant social licence constructs. 2.1. Participants Respondents for this study were recruited using an online research panel to obtain a representative sample of the Australian population. To recruit participants, a marke

Participation in the study was voluntary for members of the research panel and par- ticiaptns were awarded points (redeemable for monetary vouchers) from the market research company for participation. In total, 3103 re- spondents completed the surv

This sample size was chosen to achieve sufficient statistical power for the planned analyses, which extended beyond the analysis presented in this research. The sample was representative of the Australia population with respect to gender, a

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: the Australia population with respect to gender, age and location of residence (see Walton et al. [59] for more information regarding the demographic characteristics of the sample). Half the sample was currently employed (50.1 %) and over a third (35.6 %) of the sample owned solar panels at home. Most of the respondents completed high school (84 %) and over a third had a uni- versity degree (i.e.,

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: and over a third had a uni- versity degree (i.e., a bachelors and/or postgraduate degree; 37.6 %). 2.2. Materials and procedure Before being presented with questions assessing attitudes and beliefs regarding solar farms, all participants were presented with a hypothet- ical scenario about a planned solar farm project. Information regarding the planned solar project was presented in a mock letter

In all scenarios, participants were told about the plan the build a solar farm near their town of residence, 10 kms away from the nearest residential house. Some aspects of the solar farm were experimentally manipulated (a 3

text: he solar farm were experimentally manipulated (a 3 ×2 ×2 design) to test whether specific solar farm characteristics influenced acceptance or other psychological factors of interest. These experimental conditions had little or no effect on social acceptance or any of the other psycho- logical constructs assessed in the survey [59]. As such, the experiment conditions were not included as confoundin

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: eriment conditions were not included as confounding variables in the subsequent analyses. Solar farm acceptance, underlying social licence factors, and other psychological factors of interest were assessed using an online survey instrument. The survey instrument was created as part of a larger research project so not all the items were used in this study (see Walton et al. [59] for a full copy of

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: study (see Walton et al. [59] for a full copy of the survey instrument). Informed by the theory and empirical research, multiple items were created to assess a solar farm acceptance and a range of relevant social licence factors (e.g., perceived benefits, impacts and social trust). All these survey items were assessed using seven-point Likert scales, and scores on the items were summed and averag

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives

Seven-point scales were chosen to increase the sensitivity of the measure (i.e., capture more nuanced responses) and to increase the variability of responses for the analysis [60]. Most of these scales used to assess social licence factors were informed by past research. Some items were reverse sc

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ormed by past research. Some items were reverse scored before summing so that higher scores reflected greater levels of a construct (e.g., higher scores on social acceptance reflects greater sup- port for the proposed solar farm project). Demographic factors were not included in the analysis as they were, for the most part, unrelated to the psycho- logical constructs assessed (see

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: d to the psycho- logical constructs assessed (see Ref. [59]). This study also assessed factors that have not been included in past survey research. As discussed in the introduction, this study aimed to explore the extent to which broader beliefs about the energy transition influenced solar farm acceptance. As beliefs about the energy transition have not been comprehensively assessed in past resear

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: e not been comprehensively assessed in past research, 17 new items were created to assess these beliefs. To reduce the number of variables for subsequent analysis, a principal component analysis was used. The results of the principal component analysis suggested a four-factor so- lution. These factors were labelled ‘role for renewables’ in the energy transition, ‘role for gas’ in the transition, ‘

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: gy transition, ‘role for gas’ in the transition, ‘role for fossil fuel exports’ during the transition, and ‘willingness to pay’ or trade-off for energy transition (see Ref. [59] for the item factor loadings and the phrasing of each items). All variables were treated, and therefore modelled, as continuous variables in the subsequent analysis. 2.3. Data analysis method The data were analysed using u

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ata analysis method The data were analysed using undirected network models to explore the conditional associations between the variables of interest. While still relatively novel when applied to human data, the network approach has been used in other contexts to explain attitudes [62], psychopathology [63], personality [64] and intelligence [65]. Applied research has also used this approach to exp

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: pplied research has also used this approach to explain attitudes towards plastic [66], vaccine decisions [67], exercise behaviour [68], environmental related beliefs [69] and responses to the COVID-19 pandemic [70]. However, to the authors’ knowledge, this is the first time that a network modelling approach has been used to explore how psychological factors influence local acceptance of an energy

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: l factors influence local acceptance of an energy technology. This innovative approach can shed light on the complex factors that drive acceptance of energy technologies, which is crucial for effectively implementing sustainable energy solutions in communities around the world. Estimating undirected network models was selected as an analysis approach for two main reasons. First, network modelling

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ch for two main reasons. First, network modelling is well suited for exploratory analysis. Whether specific variables (i.e., nodes) will be connected and the direction of the relationships do not need to be specified a priori when estimating a network model. This is useful when a strong theory does not exist, and the goal is to, instead, identify robust phenomena (e.g., conditional associations be

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: obust phenomena (e.g., conditional associations between explanatory factors). As argued in the introduction, there are many different factors that may influence the acceptance of large-scale solar, and the mechanism by which these factors influence acceptance are often unclear. A good example of an unclear causal mechanism is the association between trust, the perceived effects of an energy techno

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: n trust, the perceived effects of an energy technology and acceptance. Some research argue that trust causes differences in risk/ benefit perception and, in turn, causes acceptance [34]. Other work suggests that the direction runs the other way: beliefs about impacts and benefits causes changes in trust and, in turn, acceptance [26,35]. Other explanatory factors like fairness and relationship qual

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: natory factors like fairness and relationship quality are also hypothesised to influence acceptance through different mechanisms depending on the theory [20,26,35]. As such, a modelling approach that relaxes the assumption of causality and specific pathways is useful in this context and can assist future theory development and hypothesis generation. The second reason for using an undirected networ

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: . The second reason for using an undirected network analysis approach is that these models do not assume acyclicity. That is, undi- rected network models do not assume that causality only flows in one direction (i.e., there are no feedback loops in the model). Not assuming acyclicity is useful in this context as many of the associations between psychological factors may be caused by bidirectional

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: chological factors may be caused by bidirectional effects, or by un- observed (latent) variables. For example, perceived impacts may covary with perceived benefits due to independent causal effects that each 5 R e n e w a b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e v i e w s 189(2024)114029 variable has on the other, or due to an unobserved latent variable (e.g., an implicit attitude towards

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: atent variable (e.g., an implicit attitude towards solar technology; [40,71]). Using a modelling approach that does not assume acyclicity, and therefore not ruling out feedback loops and the presence of latent variables, makes it easier to identify such effects to refine existing theories and inform the design of future confirmatory research. Undirected network models also offer a unique perspecti

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ected network models also offer a unique perspective of prac- tical importance by allowing variables to exert simultaneous influence on multiple others. Most statistical models applied in the context of understanding social acceptance focus on a singular dependent variable and thus obscure the intricate interconnections among independent variables. This approach proves especially advantageous when

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: This approach proves especially advantageous when exploring complex acceptance phenomena, as variables can propagate effects through a web of intricate associations that evade clear visuali- zation via conventional methods like linear regression. By modelling variable associations in this manner, it is possible to ascertain which variables have the greatest influence across the entire network. Th

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: e greatest influence across the entire network. These insights carry significant practical implications for policy and decision- making, as it is possible to determine the most influential variables within a system, rather than fixating on single bivariate associations. Gaussian Graphical Models (or partial correlation networks) were estimated using the ‘bootnet’ [72] package in RStudio. A Gaussia

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: g the ‘bootnet’ [72] package in RStudio. A Gaussian Graphical Model is a type of undirected network model that assumes a multivariate normal distribution. In its simplest form (without regular- isation or pruning of edges), a Gaussian Graphical Model represents bivariate partial correlation coefficients between all pairs of variables included in the model, conditioning on all other variables. In t

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: e model, conditioning on all other variables. In this study, network models were estimated using the mixed graphical model [73] function inside bootnet, which uses nodewise regressions and the lasso regularisation penalty to reduce spurious edges (i.e., weak coefficients). The nodewise estimation approach was chosen so that the explained variability in sample (i.e., R2) could be calculated for eac

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: y in sample (i.e., R2) could be calculated for each node in the network. Graphical representation of the results of the undirected network models were created using the qgraph package [74]. When plotted, the nodes in graphs represent each variable included in the analysis, and the links or ‘edges’ (associations been variables) can be interpreted as par- tial correlations or scaled regression coeff

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: par- tial correlations or scaled regression coefficients (see Ref. [75] for a summary of how partial correlations correspond to regression co- efficients). The thickness of the edges represents the strength of the regularised regression coefficient between two variables (nodes) after controlling for all other variables in the network. In other words, a thicker edges represent higher regression co

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ds, a thicker edges represent higher regression coefficients. Another way to interpret the network graphs is to think of each node as a distinct dependent (outcome) variable in a multiple linear regression model with all other nodes being independent (predictor) variables. The spring layout was specified to create the plot, which uses the Fruchterman-reingold algorithm [76] to determine spacing be

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: an-reingold algorithm [76] to determine spacing between nodes in a way that reflects the relative strength of associations [74]. Put simply, nodes that have stronger associations are placed closer together in the graph. The colour saturation and width of edges are scaled with reference to the strength of the coefficients. 3. Solar acceptance network model Fig. 1 shows the results of the first undirected network model using all of the variables shown in Table 2. The selection of explanatory var- iables to include in the model was primarily informed by commonly applied theoretical models explaining community acceptance of energ

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: al models explaining community acceptance of energy- related projects [26,35]. Although there are some differences between these theoretical models regarding the mechanisms that lead to greater community acceptance, they all specify the importance of perceived impacts, perceived benefits, distributional fairness, procedural fairness, governance, trust, and relationship quality. The whole sample wa

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ust, and relationship quality. The whole sample was used to estimate the network model. In Fig. 1, the amount of shaded area around each node represents the amount of variability in that node that is explained by all the other nodes in the network (i.e., R2). As shown in Fig. 1, roughly 75 % of the variability in the social acceptance of a local solar farm can be explained by the other variables i

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: lar farm can be explained by the other variables in the model. The core results of the analysis indicate that societal benefits, distributional fairness, and support for the energy transition narrative, all have strong direct associations with acceptance. General support for renewables is directly linked to positive feelings, while concerns about the negative impacts of solar farms indirectly af-

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: he negative impacts of solar farms indirectly af- fects acceptance through reported feelings. Distribution fairness and support for renewables predict acceptance directly and indirectly through perceived societal benefits. Beliefs about fossil fuel exports and ’peaking’ gas have more distant, indirect associations with acceptance, primarily via their correlations with worldviews and the broader su

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ir correlations with worldviews and the broader sup- port for renewables. Social factors like trust in the industry, governance, relationship quality, and procedural fairness, while having limited direct connections with acceptance, are indirectly associated via other variables. The degree to which one node effects other nodes in the network was examined by calculating a strength measure for each

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: amined by calculating a strength measure for each node using the qgraph package [74]. Node strength is calculated by the summing the edge weights (i.e., regression coefficients) that each node shares with directly connected nodes. As shown in Fig. 1, perceived societal benefits has several direct and relatively strong connections with other nodes, which is why it has high node strength (as shown i

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: which is why it has high node strength (as shown in Fig. 2). Conversely, Fig. 1. Discussion This research explored the socio-psychological factors that influence local solar farm acceptance using a network modelling approach. In accordance with past social acceptance research, perceived benefits, feelings and distributional fairness were all directly associated with local solar farm acceptance [20,24,

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ssociated with local solar farm acceptance [20,24,26]. However, unlike similar research in other energy domains, other social factors (e.g., trust and relationship quality) did not have direct associations with acceptance and had rela- tively weak indirect effects via typical mediating variables (e.g., perceived benefits and impacts). The findings also suggest that support for renewable energy in

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: also suggest that support for renewable energy in general (i.e. the energy transition narrative) is an important factor helping to explain local solar farm acceptance. This section will expand on these main empirical findings and outline some directions for future research with reference to relevant theory. The discussion will end by highlighting some of the study’s limitations and summarising the

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ome of the study’s limitations and summarising the main implications. 4.1. Broader beliefs about the energy transition One novel finding in this research was the relatively strong associ- ation between local solar farm acceptance and general support for renewable energy. Support for renewables had one of the strongest bivariate correlations with acceptance and was directly related to both acceptan

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ceptance and was directly related to both acceptance and positive feelings when controlling for all other variables in the first network model. One explanation for this finding is that people tend to think about solar farms, and their benefits, in a broader, more abstract way. Put another way, it seems that local solar farm acceptance is strongly influenced by a positive attitude towards re- newab

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: nfluenced by a positive attitude towards re- newables in general compared to a more considered assessment of the local impacts (both positive and negative) of a solar farm. This expla- nation is supported by past research showing that people tend to think about renewable energy technologies at an abstract level, which tends to result in a positive attitude [4,9]. As other research highlights, a po

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: attitude [4,9]. It was found that believing that the envi- ronment is fragile, and will only be protected if there are large changes in human behaviour, was strongly associated with support for renew- ables in the network models. Although conditional associations in the network do not infer causal effects, theory would suggest that the ‘fragile’ worldview has a causal effect on other factors as it is a more fundamental attitude. The findings, overall, suggest that those who endorse this ‘fragile’ worldview tend to be more accepting of local solar farms. It is, however, yet to be seen how strong this effect remains when people have more exposure to local effects of solar farm developments. The s

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: to local effects of solar farm developments. The second network model (Fig. 3) reveals how the ‘fragile’ world- view relates with specific beliefs about the energy transition. Fig. 3 shows that thinking the environment is fragile is most strongly associ- ated with two specific beliefs about the role of renewables. Namely, the belief that Australia should be transitioning faster and the belief that

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: should be transitioning faster and the belief that renewables will reduce greenhouse gas emissions. As shown in Fig. 4, these two beliefs have some of the strongest effects on all other nodes in the network. The finding suggests that these two beliefs, which seem to be shaped by a specific worldview, have a strong influence on people’s overall attitude toward renewables and the energy transition m

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: tude toward renewables and the energy transition more broadly. Other beliefs about the potential direct effects of using more renewables (e.g., energy reliability, energy cost and employment op- portunities) have a relatively weak influence on the network. These findings, again, support the idea that how people think about renew- ables in the abstract primarily shapes their overall attitude toward

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: act primarily shapes their overall attitude towards them. Although some broader beliefs about the renewable energy transi- tion seem to help explain local acceptance, others seem less important. Another interesting finding was that willingness to make trade-offs was quite isolated in the network models. This is a surprising result as strong support for renewables should be reflected in an increase

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: for renewables should be reflected in an increased willingness to make personal sacrifices if required (i.e., pay more for electricity or experience more blackouts). This finding suggests that while people tend to have a positive attitude towards renewables, such an attitude may not necessarily translate into behaviour that will facilitate a faster renew- able energy transition (e.g., willingness

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: r renew- able energy transition (e.g., willingness to pay more for energy if 8 M. Scovell et al. necessary). Future research is needed to test whether increases in price, or more unreliable energy supply, leads to lower levels of support for renewables. Such research would have significant implications for en- ergy policy. Beliefs about the role of gas and fossil fuel exports were also quite disti

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: gas and fossil fuel exports were also quite distinct in the network models. This finding suggests that beliefs about the role for renewables are uninfluenced by beliefs about other com- ponents of Australia’s current energy system. For example, believing that there is a role for gas (if it does not delay renewables) was unrelated to expressed support for renewables. This is another surprising out

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ort for renewables. This is another surprising outcome as people with pro-environmental beliefs tend be more supportive of renewables, but less supportive of fossil fuels [5]. This may be due to the ambivalent role of gas as both a fossil fuel and a ‘peaking’ energy source which can support a transition to renewables. The findings also suggest that if the continued use of fossil fuels does not imp

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: if the continued use of fossil fuels does not impede the development of renewable energy technologies, views about the role of fossil fuels will have a limited effect on attitudes towards renewables and, in turn, local solar farms. 4.2. title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: s solar farms was the direct link between perceived societal benefits and local acceptance. The results suggest that support for re- newables in general informs people’s views about the societal benefits of solar farms, which in turn influence local acceptance. However, perceived local benefits, while associated with societal benefits and distributional fairness, had no direct link with local sola

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ional fairness, had no direct link with local solar farm accep- tance. It is likely that those who agree that solar farms will bring about societal benefits also tend to agree that they will bring about local benefits even though this has no direct effect on acceptance. Again, it seems that more abstract views about the wider benefits of renewables and solar farms are the primary beliefs informing

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: and solar farms are the primary beliefs informing people’s expressed support for a local solar farm. The strong association between feelings, impacts and acceptance also warrants further investigation. While distributional fairness, role for renewables and social benefits all have direct associations with both acceptance and feelings, impacts only seems to associate with accep- tance via feelings

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: seems to associate with accep- tance via feelings (i.e., no direct effect). As perceived impacts is quite isolated from the other direct predictors of acceptance, it is possible that people’s attitude towards solar farms informed their expressed concern about the local impacts (i.e., the causality runs the other way). Put another way, if people like the idea of using renewables they will have a p

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ke the idea of using renewables they will have a positive attitude towards solar farms and, therefore, anticipate that the impacts will be low. The causal relationship is also supported by the theory behind the affect heuristic in that the liking or disliking of an object informs one’s explicit beliefs about that object [40,41]. For example, any feelings associated from negative imagery may direct

However, the studies that have found these effects tend to be conducted in regions where people have experience living near energy-related projects [26,35]. text: develop attitudes towards them. As most Australians have not experienced living near a solar farm, most would not have strong beliefs about the social actors involved or the gover- nance practices. Overall, the results suggest that people are not yet considering the social context in which solar farms will be built and operated and, instead, tend to rely more on their general attitude to- wards re

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives

The findings in this research should be contextualised in the broader setting of Australia’s energy transition progress. As there is a limited number of people currently living near solar farms in Australia, partic- ipants in this study were asked to share their views about living near a

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: re asked to share their views about living near a hypothetical utility-scale solar farm. There are known limitations to eliciting realistic responses to hypothetical scenarios [77] and not hav- ing real-world experience living near a solar farm may have made it difficult for respondents to consider the impact of contextual factors. An inability to fully understand and imagine local impacts may als

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: fully understand and imagine local impacts may also explain why support for renewables seemed to have a stronger influence in the network models than the perceived local effects of solar farms. Most people, without having first-hand experience, may not know what the consequences of solar farms are and how they would affect their lives. In the future, research using hypothetical scenarios to elicit

title: The impacts of consumer-funded renewable support schemes in the UK: From the perspective of consumers or the electricity sector?
text: EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) started in 2005 is the first major carbon trading market covering around 11,000 installations (power stations and industrial plants) across EU Member states and airlines operating internally.16 The EU ETS covers around 40% of the EU’s greenhouse gas emissions, and it was the crucial legislation that contributed to the reduction of GHG emissions by 31% below 199

text: to the reduction of GHG emissions by 31% below 1990 levels in 2020, exceeding the target of 20% [58]. Further, in 2020, the target was extended to 55% by 2030, compared to 1990 levels [59]. The EU ETS adopted the cap-and-trade system, in which a cap is set on the total amount of certain greenhouse gases that can be emitted. By reducing the cap gradually, the system ensures that total emissions fal

title: The impacts of consumer-funded renewable support schemes in the UK: From the perspective of consumers or the electricity sector?
text: ually, the system ensures that total emissions fall over time. Each allowance gives the holder the right to emit: one tonne of carbon dioxide (CO2) or the equivalent amount of other greenhouse gases, measured as tonne of carbon dioxide equivalent (tCO2e). Each year, an installation should surrender adequate allowances according to its emissions, and penalties are imposed on any quantity of allowan

title: The impacts of consumer-funded renewable support schemes in the UK: From the perspective of consumers or the electricity sector?
text: d penalties are imposed on any quantity of allowances missed. After paying the penalty, installations are still obliged to sur- render missed allowances in the following year. If an installation reduces its emissions, its spare allowances can be sold to another installation that needs additional allowances. The value of allowances imposes costs on GHG emissions and encourages investment in low-car

title: The impacts of consumer-funded renewable support schemes in the UK: From the perspective of consumers or the electricity sector?

The application to the UK As the UK was dependent on imports of fossil fuels and included in the ET ETS, the framework discussed in Section 3 suggests that the sector should be better off from fewer imports and emissions from the transi- tion from fossil fuels to renewables. Meanwhile, as Section 3.3.2 ex- plains, to understand the impacts of the

text: 3.3.2 ex- plains, to understand the impacts of the renewable support schemes on the UK electricity sector, we should identify the sources of fossil fuels that have been replaced by renewable electricity in order to find the cost coefficients related to imports of fossil fuels and GHG emissions. If the analysis is based on hourly data, as peaking gas-fired plants are switched off when wind farms be

Nonetheless, we examine the structural change in the generation mix to see if this conclusion still holds on a longer horizon. 4.1. title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ew coherence lens for advancing 1. Introduction Governments across a range of countries are seeking to coordinate the buildout of new large-scale renewable energy infrastructure through designated renewable energy zones (REZs) or similar frameworks, including in Australia, India, the Philippines, South Africa and the United States [1]. While there has been extensive interest in the op- portunities

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: has been extensive interest in the op- portunities that renewable energy offers to decentralise energy pro- duction away from fossil fuel power plants, REZs represent a partial recentralisation of power generation, thus interacting with other cen- tripetal dynamics such as competitive auctions that favour utility-scale renewable energy projects [2]. As one report notes, “REZs are the equivalent of

Accordingly, understanding how the establishment and implementation of REZs affect the pursuit of other policy objectives (including social equity and the wellbeing of rural communities) is an important step towards robust, fair and pub- licly supported energy policy [8]. This article asks: (a) what synergies an

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: licy [8]. This article asks: (a) what synergies and tensions arise between renewable energy and social equity in the establishment and early implementation of REZs and how are those synergies and tensions being managed so far, and (b) what political factors drive synergies and ten- sions between these policy objectives and how could they be managed more effectively? We explore these questions thro

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: more effectively? We explore these questions through the lens of policy coherence, which can be understood as an approach to policy-making that minimises conflicts among policy objectives and maximises syn- ergies among them [9,10]. A coherent approach in this context would mean that the expansion of renewable energy simultaneously advances social equity (thus enabling REZs to become “regional eq

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: equity (thus enabling REZs to become “regional equity zones” too), while an incoherent approach would mean that renewable energy expansion exacerbates inequity or fails to alleviate it. We present a case study of renewable energy expansion in New South Wales (NSW), which is Australia's most populous state and a first mover on REZs nationally [11]. Our analysis focuses on early implementation of t

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: Our analysis focuses on early implementation of the NSW Elec- tricity Infrastructure Roadmap (“the Roadmap”), a policy framework launched by the NSW Government in 2020 that aims to expand the state's renewable energy capacity, primarily through the construction of REZs, while also pursuing a range of equity-related objectives. Section 2 sets out the analytical lens of policy coherence that we emp

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: he analytical lens of policy coherence that we employ in the article. Section 3 provides an overview of the case study context and methods. Section 4 identifies areas of coherence and inco- herence in the early implementation of the NSW Roadmap. Our analysis draws on government policies, reviews and media reports, as well as interviews and focus groups involving forty participants. We identify sev

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: oups involving forty participants. We identify several areas of incoherence, including limited progress in ensuring that First Nations people, low-income groups and rural communities can harness the benefits of the energy transition despite a range of regula- tory measures intended to facilitate this, as well as some areas of coherence, including enhanced benefit-sharing mechanisms and bolstering

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: nhanced benefit-sharing mechanisms and bolstering institutional capacity for community engagement. In section 5 we employ a recently developed framework for analysing political drivers of coherence and incoherence spanning ideas, institutions and interests [10]. We identify several drivers of incoherence, including political polarisation over energy policy, limited inter-agency coordi- nation and

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: y policy, limited inter-agency coordi- nation and scale mismatches that disincentivise community-owned en- ergy initiatives. Drivers of coherence include cross-party dialogue, strengthening institutional capacity, and incentives for project de- velopers to invest in community engagement and benefit-sharing. Sec- tion 6 then discusses the findings and draws out a range of policy implications. Throu

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: nd draws out a range of policy implications. Through this analysis, the article aims both to advance theory, by demonstrating the usefulness of a policy coherence lens for revealing, explaining and managing synergies and trade-offs in energy policy, and to contribute to the emerging body of empirical evidence on the socio- economic consequences of REZs. More broadly, the article also aims to furth

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: REZs. More broadly, the article also aims to further scholarly understanding of opportunities and constraints for advancing energy justice and social equity [12], as well as the politics of scale around energy infrastructure [13,14]. 2. Analytical framework: policy coherence and social equity in energy transitions 2.1. A policy coherence lens on energy transitions and social equity The need for en

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ergy transitions and social equity The need for energy policy to manage trade-offs among multiple 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c ie n c e 130 (2025) 104415 policy objectives is often conceptualised as a “trilemma” that requires balancing the security, sustainability and affordability of energy supply [15].2While this concept is useful insofar as it highlights the challenge of bala

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ful insofar as it highlights the challenge of balancing multiple priorities in energy policy, conventional framings of the energy trilemma have been criticised for downplaying other important values or objectives, including procedural and recognitional justice, and other facets of wellbeing that are affected by energy, including community cohesion, autonomy and attachment to place (see e.g. [16]).

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: autonomy and attachment to place (see e.g. [16]). Thus, there is value in exploring more comprehensive and flexible frameworks for analysing trade-offs and synergies. A policy coherence perspective on energy policy may also complement an energy conflict lens [6] while offering additional insights, as it can reveal areas of policy incoherence that may produce adverse effects even if they do not ma

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: may produce adverse effects even if they do not manifest in outright conflict, while also highlighting areas of synergy as well as areas of tension or conflict. Policy coherence has become increasingly cited as both a desirable feature of contemporary governance and as an analytical lens for ana- lysing trade-offs and synergies. The United Nations Sustainable Devel- opment Goals, for example, incl

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: Sustainable Devel- opment Goals, for example, include a target calling upon states to “Enhance policy coherence for sustainable development” (target 17.14). Policy coherence for sustainable development can be defined as an “approach and policy tool to systematically integrate the social, eco- nomic and environmental dimensions of sustainable development at all stages of policy making” [17]. A grow

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: pment at all stages of policy making” [17]. A growing body of literature has begun to apply a policy coherence lens to the context of energy, but much of this work focuses on the European Union [8] with limited coverage of other regions. Moreover, there has been limited systematic investigation of the factors that enable or constrain policy coherence in energy policy, as we elaborate in section 2.

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ce in energy policy, as we elaborate in section 2.2 below. At various stages of the policy process from agenda-setting and policy formulation to implementation, (in)coherence may arise (a) be- tween different policy objectives (horizontal [in]coherence, e.g. between renewable energy expansion and local employment) at one level of governance, or (b) in the pursuit of a single objective by actors or

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: in the pursuit of a single objective by actors or in- stitutions at different levels (vertical [in]coherence, e.g. where federal and state policies on energy overlap, clash or reinforce one another) [8]. Horizontal and vertical dimensions may also interact (e.g. where a na- tional government's pursuit of renewable energy supports or obstructs the ability of a subnational government to encourage l

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ability of a subnational government to encourage local employ- ment). We mainly address horizontal coherence at the subnational/state level between two sets of policy objectives: (a) climate change mitiga- tion through the expansion of renewable energy (“energy objectives”); and (b) the pursuit of social equity (“equity objectives”).3 We refer to social equity (or in brief “equity”) in the broad s

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ocial equity (or in brief “equity”) in the broad sense of “fair treatment and access to opportunities for all” [18], accounting for differentiated needs and historical legacies/injustices in the circum- stances of both individuals and social groups [19]. We understand eq- uity in both procedural and substantive terms, comprising both (a) the ability of individuals and social groups (particularly m

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: y of individuals and social groups (particularly marginalised or vulnerable groups) to be recognised and participate in decision-making as well as (b) the fair distribution of the social and economic burdens and benefits associated with the energy transition among individuals and social groups [20].4Building on key equity concerns identified in previous research on energy justice [21] and on REZs

text: vious research on energy justice [21] and on REZs [4] as well as an inductive approach informed by initial interviews for this project, our 2 The latter objective is sometimes characterised as “equity” but often measured in relatively narrow terms of affordability and access (e.g. [15]). 3 We briefly touch on interactions between horizontal and vertical coherence in section 5.3.2. 4 Both dimension

However, space precludes a detailed discussion of the relationship between social equity and these related terms, or of other dimensions of equity and justice (e.g. recognition). 2 J. text: nition). 2 J. Pickering and P. Chalaye analysis focuses on procedural and distributive equity for three social groups: (i) rural communities in REZs, and within them (ii) low-income and marginalised groups, and (iii) First Nations people (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people). For reasons of space we exclude detailed discussion of equity for people outside REZs, while acknowledging that bo
