text: r people outside REZs, while acknowledging that both the upfront costs of REZ construction and the longer-term savings from the shift to renewable energy will have significant implications for consumers across the electricity network via their effects on electricity prices (INT19G; INT20B; INT31N) [22]. 2.2. Drivers of coherence and incoherence The limited literature on drivers of policy (in)coher

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: limited literature on drivers of policy (in)coherence has tended to focus on the role of institutional factors such as interdepartmental coordination or mainstreaming and policy integration strategies [10,23]. However, a narrow institutional or technocratic focus may overlook the importance of political factors in facilitating coherence or obstructing it. Recent research has sought to gain greate

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ting it. Recent research has sought to gain greater conceptual clarity about political drivers of (in)coherence by employing a typology that distinguishes three categories of drivers: ideas, institutions and in- terests [10]. Ideas refer to “claims about descriptions of the world, causal relationships, or the normative legitimacy of certain actors” [24]. In- stitutions refer to “a cluster of right

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: [24]. In- stitutions refer to “a cluster of rights, rules, and decision making pro- cedures that gives rise to a social practice, assigns roles to participants in the practice, and guides interactions among occupants of these roles” [25]. Interests are understood as “material or advocacy considerations of specific sectors and actors related to pursuing their objectives” [10]. While existing liter

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: suing their objectives” [10]. While existing literature has recognised the role of these categories in influencing the stringency of energy policy (see e.g. [26] on ideas and [27] on institutions and interests), less work has been done to date on how these categories may drive (in)coherence in energy policy. Strambo et al. [28], for example, contend that factors such as framing (an idea- tional fa

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: d that factors such as framing (an idea- tional factor), political will and market conditions (interest-based fac- tors) may influence coherence between energy security and climate policy objectives, but their analysis focuses primarily on measuring coherence rather than investigating its causes. Section 5 applies the three-part typology outlined above to the case study context. 3. Case study over

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: bove to the case study context. 3. Case study overview and methods 3.1. Case study selection and policy context 3.1.1. Renewable energy and socioeconomic inequity in Australia Australia serves as a useful case study for understanding the socio- economic dynamics of renewable energy zones as it is one of the countries, along with the United States, that has seen the greatest level of policy interes

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: that has seen the greatest level of policy interest and activity in REZ models [1].5Australia is one of the world's largest exporters of fossil fuels. Regarding the three groups outlined in section 2.1 on which our equity analysis focuses: (i) on average, people living in rural communities in Australia have lower incomes and lower levels of social support than people living in metropolitan areas [

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: support than people living in metropolitan areas [30,31]; (ii) Australia has a relatively low level of socio-economic inequality by world standards [32], but inequality re- mains a major issue; and (iii) First Nations people continue to experience poorer health and well-being and higher levels of energy insecurity than non-Indigenous Australians while grappling with legacies of disposses- sion and

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ile grappling with legacies of disposses- sion and intergenerational trauma [29,33,34]. In Australia, responsibility for energy and climate policy is shared primarily between state and federal governments, with local govern- ments having a subsidiary role. To accelerate progress towards its goal of 5 A key driver for the introduction of REZs in Australia was an independent review of the electricit

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: tralia was an independent review of the electricity market in 2017 led by Chief Scientist Alan Finkel, which drew on the example of Competitive Renewable Energy Zones in Texas [29]. 3 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c ie n c e 130 (2025) 104415 achieving net zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050, the Federal Government aims to generate 82 % of the country's electricity from renewable s

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: 82 % of the country's electricity from renewable sources by 2030 [35]. The eight states and territories (including New South Wales) have an associated range of short-term climate and renewable energy targets. While electricity grids have largely developed at the state level, electricity policy is now an area of intense interaction between the Federal Government and the states. Australia's National

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: al Government and the states. Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) covers all states and territories except the Northern Territory and Western Australia. The NEM is regulated through a co- ordinated set of state and territory legislation and policy (notably the National Electricity Law and National Electricity Rules) as well as in- stitutions such as the Australian Energy Market Operator

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ons such as the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO), which has a key role in coordinating the development of REZs across the NEM, including via its biennial Integrated System Plans [36]. All juris- dictions participating in the NEM are establishing REZs, supported by state-specific legislation and policy. Federal-state coordination on en- ergy is facilitated by intergovernmental bodies such a

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: is facilitated by intergovernmental bodies such as the Energy and Climate Change Ministerial Council. 3.1.2. The New South Wales Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap Of Australia's eight subnational jurisdictions (states and territories), NSW has the largest population (including the largest Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander population) and the largest economy [37]. Most of the state's electric

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: largest economy [37]. Most of the state's electricity infrastructure – with the exception of regional distribution networks – has been partially or wholly privatised since 2010 ([38]). Despite having the smallest share of renewable energy generation after Queensland, and the largest total energy consumption in the country [39], NSW generates the largest amount of renewable energy and hosts the lar

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: rgest amount of renewable energy and hosts the largest number of large-scale renewable energy projects [40]. In 2020 the NSW Government launched a new 20-year policy framework for renewable energy expansion (the NSW Electricity Infra- structure Roadmap; hereafter “the Roadmap”) – and associated legisla- tion (the Electricity Infrastructure Investment [EII] Act 2020). The primary purpose of the Roa

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: nt [EII] Act 2020). The primary purpose of the Roadmap is to drive renewable energy expansion – particularly to avert the risk of energy shortages following recent and impending closures of ageing coal-fired power plants – and thereby accelerate progress towards the state's greenhouse gas emissions reduction targets. This is to be achieved primarily through underwriting of new large-scale solar an

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: y through underwriting of new large-scale solar and wind generation, transmission and energy storage [11]. While pre-existing planning legislation and policy continue to regulate the construction of renewable energy infrastructure across the state (including outside REZ boundaries), the EII Act is a distinctive development in that it establishes a legislative basis for constructing REZs in designa

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: legislative basis for constructing REZs in designated areas of the state. Developers (or “proponents”) are incentivised to propose projects in REZs through the opportunity to tender for rights to access the grid and for Long Term Energy Service Agreements (LTESAs), which aim to provide minimum revenue streams for investors while protecting consumers from excessively high costs [41]. The EII Act's

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: s from excessively high costs [41]. The EII Act's first objective (“to improve the affordability, reliability, security and sustainability of electricity supply”) recalls the energy tri- lemma [38], but this is only one of eleven objectives. Notably, the Act contains several objectives relating to distributive equity, including promoting local education, training, industry and jobs (section 3(1)).

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ation, training, industry and jobs (section 3(1)). While the Act includes objectives aligned with procedural equity to “foster local community support” for investment in renewables (section 3(1)(d)) and to “promote consultation and negotiation” with Aboriginal landowners (section 3(2)(b), it does not foreground broader community engagement requirements [42]. The government-owned Energy Cor- porati

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ents [42]. The government-owned Energy Cor- poration of NSW (EnergyCo) is tasked with coordinating the overall rollout of REZs in NSW, of which five have been declared as of mid- 3 J. Pickering and P. Chalaye 2025.6 Much of the present analysis focuses on the Central-West Orana REZ (CWO REZ), which was the first to be declared in 2021. This REZ covers an area of roughly 20,000 km2 on the lands of

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ers an area of roughly 20,000 km2 on the lands of the Wiradjuri, Wailwan and Kamilaroi people, surrounding several regional centres including Dubbo and Mudgee. The network capacity of this REZ was initially estimated at 3 GW, and in 2023 was doubled to 6 GW due to increasing demand for electricity in the state. During the case study period (2021(cid:0) 2023), the CWO REZ was still in its early sta

text: d:0) 2023), the CWO REZ was still in its early stages of development, with much work focusing on initial community engagement, scoping of individual infrastructure projects and the first rounds of tenders for LTESAs.7 3.2. Methods This article is based on a mixed-methods analysis comprising docu- mentary analysis, focus groups, interviews and event observation. The documentary analysis draws on cl

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: observation. The documentary analysis draws on climate, energy and equity policies at the federal and NSW levels, as well as a range of academic literature, think tank and NGO reports, press articles and stakeholder submissions to policy processes. We also held thirty interviews and two focus groups between February 2021 and February 2023 with forty participants, principally federal, state/territ

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ty participants, principally federal, state/territory and local government officials, non- governmental organisations, First Nations organisations, universities and think tanks, and renewable energy project developers. Appendix A1 provides further details about interview participants. Appendix A2 in- cludes a template interview script. The case study included a short period of fieldwork in the Mud

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: dy included a short period of fieldwork in the Mudgee region within the CWO REZ in February 2023, and participant observation in an end-of-year briefing organised by EnergyCo (online, December 2022) and a stakeholder consultation on the First Nations Clean Energy Strategy (Canberra, August 2023). Finally, a multistakeholder concluding workshop held in Sydney in September 2023 informed this article

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: in Sydney in September 2023 informed this article and a more detailed project report [43]. The analysis mainly investigates the earlier stages of REZ imple- mentation from 2021 to 2023, although subsequent developments (up to July 2025) are noted where relevant, particularly if they corroborate or diverge from findings from the earlier period. Given that many projects were yet to be constructed b

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: en that many projects were yet to be constructed by the end of 2023, much of the evidence presented in this article is based on participants' first-hand experiences of community engagement and interactions with developers, and to a lesser extent on attitudes towards pre-existing or prospective infra- structure, with less emphasis on participants' experiences of the con- struction process itself.8S

text: xperiences of the con- struction process itself.8Studying this early period can still yield vital insights as it covers a crucial phase in which relations of trust or mistrust as well as institutional dynamics are established that may have crucial implications for later phases of implementation [45]. Policy documents, interview and focus group transcripts and obser- vational notes were coded thema

text: scripts and obser- vational notes were coded thematically using NVivo data analysis soft- ware according to policy objectives and categories and drivers of policy (in)coherence based on the framework set out in section 2. Both projects are under construction as of mid-2025. 8 Community attitudes towards renewable energy infrastructure may change before and after construction, sometimes becoming more positive [44]. 4 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c ie n c

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c ie n c e 130 (2025) 104415 4. Policy coherence in early implementation of the New South Wales Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap This section assesses evidence of (in)coherence between energy and equity objectives in the initial phase of implementing the NSW Elec- tricity Infrastructure Roadmap. The analysis is differentiated according to the two dimensio

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: is is differentiated according to the two dimensions of procedural and substantive equity set out in section 2.1 above. 4.1. Procedural equity Our analysis of procedural equity spans opportunities for authentic and inclusive public engagement at three stages of the policy process: the leadup to establishing REZs (section 4.1.1), during the early imple- mentation process (section 4.1.2) and on indi

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ple- mentation process (section 4.1.2) and on individual projects (4.1.3). We also highlight issues of coherence relating to the participation of particular social groups (4.1.4), including First Nations people (4.1.5). Initial policy implementation is coherent if efforts to expand renewable energy through the Roadmap tend to enhance procedural equity, and incoherent if those efforts either underm

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ity, and incoherent if those efforts either undermine procedural equity or fail to address existing procedural inequities. 4.1.1. Establishment of Renewable Energy Zones Decisions about where to site REZs have important flow-on effects for the communities within them (see section 4.2 below). The EII Act requires the Minister to have considered “the views of the local com- munity” in the REZ before

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: views of the local com- munity” in the REZ before declaring a zone (EII Act, section 19(4)(b) (iii)). “Investor and community support” was one of five criteria used to identify candidate REZs, in addition to factors such as potential to generate renewable energy, proximity to existing and proposed trans- mission infrastructure, and implications for agricultural land use and biodiversity conservat

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: r agricultural land use and biodiversity conservation [46]. Candidate REZs were largely identified through a geospatial mapping exercise, and extent of community sup- port was primarily derived from a separate regional planning process rather than from a tailored consultation strategy. In the CWO REZ, many community members were initially unclear about why their area had been chosen as a REZ (INT1

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: bout why their area had been chosen as a REZ (INT12N, INT20B, INT24N; see also [47], p.30). One local government official commented that the council was “blind- sided” by the announcement and that the implications of the REZ were not explained in terms that would enable the council to develop an informed position on it (INT26G; see also [4]). 4.1.2. Community outreach and engagement across the REZ

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: . Community outreach and engagement across the REZ Numerous respondents stated that community outreach and aware- ness raising in the Central-West Orana REZ had been limited in the early stages of the Roadmap (roughly late 2020 through to early 2022) (INT12N, INT17N, INT26G). They pointed out that these efforts had increased over time, partly because community groups and councils had raised concer

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: se community groups and councils had raised concerns about lack of engagement but also because EnergyCo's staffing and organisational capacity had built up and COVID-19 re- strictions had eased (INT12N, INT26G). EnergyCo conducted a range of online and in-person community outreach events and surveys [48], opened an office in Dubbo, and established community reference groups for several REZs involv

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: community reference groups for several REZs involving representatives from farming, environmental and business organisations and the broader community. However, some interviewees expressed concern about the accessibility of timely and appropriate information about the CWO REZ (INT12N, INT23N). As the Roadmap has evolved, numerous respondents cited the onset of consultation fatigue, leading to dise

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: the onset of consultation fatigue, leading to disengagement and mistrust (INT14B, INT17N, INT24N, INT26G, [49]). While engagement under the Roadmap involved a range of different avenues for participation, most of them appeared to emphasise consul- tation rather than more intensive forms of participation such as co- 4 J. Pickering and P. Chalaye design/co-production or deliberation (INT19G, [42]).

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ign/co-production or deliberation (INT19G, [42]). Two notable ex- ceptions in NSW have been (i) the involvement of First Nations groups in co-designing the First Nations guidelines9; and (ii) a community-led participatory initiative in the town of Hay to identify priorities for their involvement in the South-West REZ [51]. 4.1.3. Consultation and negotiations on individual projects Whereas broader

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: egotiations on individual projects Whereas broader community outreach across the REZs is coordi- nated by EnergyCo, consultation on individual projects is usually led by project developers. Consultation both before and after the Roadmap's establishment was reported to be highly variable, with some developers being far more thorough and inclusive than others (INT24N). Partici- pants also noted a ra

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: an others (INT24N). Partici- pants also noted a range of concerns about consultation and negotiation processes, including: insufficient consultation with landholders and neighbours at an early stage (INT17N); inadequate information about the impacts of proposed projects (INT23N) or conversely information overload (INT25N); and divisive strategies used by some developers to secure consent for hosti

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: sed by some developers to secure consent for hosting projects (INT23N; see also [49]). Several respondents mentioned the cumulative impacts of multiple projects as a major driver of community opposition and an impediment to community engagement: Some projects that did have social licence that were […] early movers now are finding that they've got community opposition because all of a sudden there'

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ommunity opposition because all of a sudden there's another project next door to their project, and […] the community that were quite happy to have this one wind farm now find themselves surrounded by wind farms or solar farms, and […] they're not happy. (INT14B; corroborated by INT15B, INT24N) In their environmental impact assessment, developers are required to show how they will address cumulati

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: re required to show how they will address cumulative impacts of multiple projects (INT22B), but some participants considered it important to take into account other stressors that their communities have experienced in recent years, including drought, bushfires, COVID-19, flooding, mice plagues and controversies over other major developments (INT25N, [47]). 4.1.4. Participation of different social

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: , [47]). 4.1.4. Participation of different social groups Respondents acknowledged the difficulty of engaging underrepre- sented or harder-to-reach social groups, including lower-income households. Several obstacles were identified, including limited internet access, feeling intimidated by the prospect of going to a com- munity consultation meeting (INT20B), limited education inhibiting people's ab

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: (INT20B), limited education inhibiting people's ability to understand information or advocate for themselves (INT09B, INT25N), and inappropriate timing of consultation events, e.g. during work hours (INT26G). Developers frequently contact local or- ganisations that involve a wide cross-section of the community and engage locally based consultants for outreach, but even with wide- ranging publicity

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: or outreach, but even with wide- ranging publicity of events, turnout is often small (INT20B, INT26G). The dynamics of consultation meetings can also reinforce existing inequalities of participation. For example, people in lower-income groups may be less confident in raising concerns than wealthier land- owners, and settings such as town hall meetings – which often have an adversarial atmosphere –

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ngs – which often have an adversarial atmosphere – may discourage women's participation (FG02R, [52]). 4.1.5. Involvement of First Nations people Respondents reported mixed views about the extent of engagement with First Nations people in implementing the NSW Roadmap. One respondent commented that there was little consideration of Aboriginal people's interests when the location of the REZs was dec

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: 's interests when the location of the REZs was decided (INT18R). Subsequently, EnergyCo worked with First Nations groups to co-design 9 Another recent example of co-design at the federal level is the development of the First Nations Clean Energy Strategy [50]. 5 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c ie n c e 130 (2025) 104415 guidelines on engagement with First Nations people on the REZs.

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: engagement with First Nations people on the REZs. Some respondents noted that in recent years the scope of consultation with First Nations communities on projects had broadened, and that some developers had begun engaging earlier (INT14B, INT20B). However, Norman et al. find that by mid-2023 there was still “limited and uneven engagement with Aboriginal communities on renewable projects in the Cen

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ginal communities on renewable projects in the Central-West Orana REZ and other declared REZs” [53]. Local Aborig- inal Lands Councils in NSW reported that “they were not prepared or fully aware of the possibilities for engaging with renewable energy op- erators or projects on their land” [53]. 4.2. Distributive equity This section addresses the potential for renewable energy expansion under the R

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: tential for renewable energy expansion under the Roadmap to produce socioeconomic benefits and impacts for local communities, encompassing broader economic consequences (4.2.1), benefit-sharing mechanisms (4.2.2), community ownership (4.2.3) and implications for First Nations people (4.2.4). Initial policy implementation is coherent if efforts to expand renewable energy are likely to enhance distr

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: xpand renewable energy are likely to enhance distributive equity, and incoherent if those efforts either exacerbate or fail to address distributive inequity. 4.2.1. Economic benefits and impacts within REZs As noted in the introduction, one important function of REZs is to concentrate – both geographically and temporally – the benefits and impacts of renewable energy development within these zones

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: of renewable energy development within these zones. This poses both opportunities and challenges for coherent and equitable policy-making and implementation. When the Roadmap was launched, EnergyCo estimated that landholders hosting projects in the CWO REZ could receive up to A$430 million in periodic lease payments (INT15B, INT25N, [49]). Broader communities may reap economic benefits through emp

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: communities may reap economic benefits through employment, local procurement and increased economic ac- tivity associated with construction (INT11G, [54]) and benefit-sharing mechanisms (see section 4.2.2 below). When launching the Roadmap, the NSW Government highlighted its potential to generate up to A$1.5 billion in lease payments and substantial employment for regional communities, with an est

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: l employment for regional communities, with an estimate of 6300 construction jobs and 2800 ongoing jobs by 2030, mostly in regional NSW in 2030 [11]. However, a range of adverse consequences could also accrue, including land use disruption (INT14B, INT26G, [49]) and visual/noise impacts (INT12N, INT21N, INT26G). Adverse economic impacts, while only briefly touched upon in the Roadmap document, wer

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: briefly touched upon in the Roadmap document, were a key concern for many interviewees, including pressure on the availability and price of housing, healthcare and other services, limited availability of workers for other needs, traffic disruption, increased costs of road maintenance and waste disposal, and local biodiversity (INT10G, INT11G, INT20B, INT21N, INT22B, INT24N, INT26G; see also [48,4

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: 0B, INT21N, INT22B, INT24N, INT26G; see also [48,49]). All of these impacts could accumulate due to the higher concentration of projects in REZs. The NSW policy framework includes a range of measures to address impacts and benefits, including: state environmental and social impact assessment requirements; additional merit criteria for developers tendering for projects in REZs covering land use/com

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ndering for projects in REZs covering land use/community impacts and regional economic development; and bodies established under the EII Act such as the Renewable Energy Sector Board and the Electricity Infrastructure Jobs Advocate that advise on how to maximise economic, training and employment benefits from REZs. EnergyCo has also con- ducted analytical work on impacts and benefits and outlined

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: lytical work on impacts and benefits and outlined opportu- nities to address them [55]. Some participants identified factors that could limit the ability of regional communities to benefit from renewable energy projects, including local skills shortages and low levels of unemployment (INT11G, INT22B, [49,54,56,57]). Accordingly, some respondents felt that the local employment and procurement targe

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: lt that the local employment and procurement targets set out in NSW guidelines were challenging (INT11G, INT22B) or even unrealistic (INT20B) given the limited local manufacturing capacity and 5 J. Pickering and P. Chalaye workforces. The NSW Renewable Energy Sector Board found in 2024 that renewable energy projects that had been awarded LTESAs were often struggling to meet local content requireme

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: e often struggling to meet local content requirements, particularly for the operations and maintenance phase [58]. While renewable energy projects in REZs could open up economic opportunities for regional areas, some groups that are under-represented in the renewable energy workforce or associated industries (including women, low-income groups and people with disabilities) may face ob- stacles in

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: people with disabilities) may face ob- stacles in harnessing these opportunities (INT11G). LTESA tenders require that proponents commit to employing at least 15 % of their workforce from underrepresented groups,10but the Renewable Energy Sector Board found in 2024 that most project proponents had only made “minimal commitments” to this end [58]. Moreover, low-income groups may experience adverse i

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: reover, low-income groups may experience adverse impacts (e.g. higher rental prices or pressure on health services) disproportionately. 4.2.2. Benefit-sharing mechanisms The NSW Government's Benefit-sharing Guideline for large-scale renewable energy projects recognises the importance of benefit- sharing mechanisms beyond individual landholder payments on the basis that “host communities, which bea

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: nts on the basis that “host communities, which bear the brunt of the changes, may not necessarily experience a proportionate level of benefits from the uptake of renewable energy” [59]. Benefit-sharing mechanisms for renewable energy schemes in NSW involve a mix of voluntary and mandatory components [49] and commonly support initiatives such as community infrastructure upgrades or sponsorship of l

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: munity infrastructure upgrades or sponsorship of local organisa- tions (INT24N). The Roadmap has incentivised voluntary benefit- sharing arrangements through tender criteria as well as mandating that operators who obtain access rights pay access fees, a portion of which will be channelled towards community and employment purposes via REZ-wide schemes overseen by EnergyCo (INT10G). Numerous respond

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: es overseen by EnergyCo (INT10G). Numerous respondents affirmed the importance of benefit-sharing mechanisms for building community support for renewable energy projects (INT12N, INT14B, INT17N, INT20B). However, some commu- nity members were concerned that monetary benefit-sharing mecha- nisms were either insufficient or inappropriate for offsetting some impacts (e.g. on landscapes or community c

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ng some impacts (e.g. on landscapes or community cohesion), and there was a degree of suspicion that developers are “buying off the community to continue doing harm” (INT24N) or that payments largely serve to placate urban electricity consumers (INT31N). REZ-wide benefit-sharing schemes offer the possibility of delivering more substantial benefits to communities (INT12N, INT24N). Ahead of the comm

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: to communities (INT12N, INT24N). Ahead of the commencement of schemes funded by access fees, the NSW Gov- ernment announced in 2023 an A$128 million Community Employment and Benefit Program in the CWO REZ, and in early 2025 announced the first A$60 million in grants under the program [60]. The largest single grants were allocated to local councils, notably to establish a training facility for the

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: notably to establish a training facility for the renewable energy workforce and housing for key workers in larger population centres, but a substantial amount of this funding also went towards upgrading infrastructure in smaller towns close to REZ projects. A much larger number of small grants was distributed among a variety of community organisations across the REZ. Of these, a small proportion

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ions across the REZ. Of these, a small proportion of grants was targeted towards First Nations groups and culturally and linguistically diverse communities, and a larger proportion towards children and young people. The Program also in- cludes a dedicated A$10 million First Nations Fund. While the sums allocated so far are fairly modest, they will increase over time as project- specific and REZ-wi

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: increase over time as project- specific and REZ-wide schemes ramp up. Overall, the allocation of funds appears to reflect (at least in part) a mix of criteria for distribution identified by NSW policies and community members in the CWO REZ, including prioritising those most affected by REZ projects while also promoting broader-based regional development (see section 5.4.2). 10 These include women,

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: pment (see section 5.4.2). 10 These include women, youth, long-term unemployed and people with characteristics defined under the Anti-Discrimination Act 1977 (NSW). 6 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c ie n c e 130 (2025) 104415 4.2.3. Community ownership and co-investment Community-owned renewable projects aim not only to augment renewable energy capacity but also to empower communitie

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ble energy capacity but also to empower communities and build social licence for the energy transition (INT14B; see also FG02R, [48,61]). While community-owned renewable projects are prominent in some countries such as Germany, Denmark and Scotland, to date there are relatively few in Australia [49], although the sector has experienced significant growth over the past decade [62]. A respondent com

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: growth over the past decade [62]. A respondent commented that the Roadmap neither blocks nor incentivises community ownership/investment models in the REZs (INT12N). In practice, however, several factors tend to reinforce the emphasis on large-scale projects run by private developers, including: limited technical capacity among communities and developers (INT17N); high financial risk for small pro

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: lopers (INT17N); high financial risk for small projects particularly during their early stages (INT17N); and regulatory barriers to people directly using the energy from community energy projects (FG02R). As of mid-2025, no community-owned energy project groups were recorded as being active in the CWO REZ [63], but at least one project developer (Squadron Energy) has launched a program to enable c

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: quadron Energy) has launched a program to enable community co- investment in its CWO REZ projects [64]. 4.2.4. Benefits and impacts for First Nations people Renewable energy projects could yield a range of benefits for First Nations people living in REZs (FG01R, FG02R, INT08B, INT18R, INT31N, [53]). Many of these benefits parallel those outlined in section 4.2.1 above, including employment, procur

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: section 4.2.1 above, including employment, procurement and benefit-sharing mechanisms. Two further aspects include improving energy reliability, especially in more remote areas [53] (see also FG01R, FG02R, INT08B, INT18R, INT31N), and economic benefits associated with hosting pro- jects on land held under land rights or Native Title legislation (INT18R). Despite these opportunities, respondents a

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: NT18R). Despite these opportunities, respondents also noted a range of challenges. Here we focus on two socio-economic aspects.11First, op- tions for siting projects directly on Land Rights Act land in NSW were often limited (INT27N) (especially in comparison with jurisdictions such as Western Australia and the Northern Territory), as this land is often marginal, fragmented and unsuitable for rene

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: often marginal, fragmented and unsuitable for renewable energy projects (e.g. due to conservation zoning; INT18R). Meanwhile, other parcels of land are subject to long-unresolved land claims (INT27N, INT28N). As of mid-2025 there were no large-scale Aboriginal-owned renewable energy projects in NSW [66]. Second, there are challenges associated with accessing employment opportunities. Aboriginal an

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: accessing employment opportunities. Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people remain underrepresented in the renewable energy workforce, comprising around 0.8 % of the total (despite making up 3.3 % of Australia's overall population: [67]). The Roadmap includes a range of mechanisms to encourage First Nations employment and pro- curement in REZ projects, including criteria in tenders for LTESA

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: projects, including criteria in tenders for LTESAs. However, most projects have fallen short of these targets [58,66], indicating that this aspect of distributive equity has been difficult to fulfil in practice. 5. Drivers of policy coherence and incoherence 5.1. Section overview In this section we investigate a range of factors that could explain the evidence of policy coherence and incoherence

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: the evidence of policy coherence and incoherence presented in the previous section, categorised across three dimensions: ideas, institutions and in- terests. For each dimension we select two drivers that emerged as most prominent in interviews and documentary analysis. Appendix A3 sum- marises the full range of drivers identified in the case study. Further analysis of each driver (including broad

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: . Further analysis of each driver (including broader systemic drivers such as 11 Projects could have a range of adverse impacts on Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander cultural heritage (INT15B, INT27N) but detailed discussion of this point is beyond the scope of this article (for further analysis see [65]). 6 J. Pickering and P. Chalaye neoliberalism and colonial legacies) can be found in [43] (

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: lism and colonial legacies) can be found in [43] (see also [68–70]). 5.2. Ideas 5.2.1. Political ideologies: polarization and common ground Ideological polarisation can drive incoherence by prioritising a narrow range of policy objectives while sidelining others [43]. Conversely, cross-party deliberation can inject greater ideological di- versity into policy formulation, albeit at the potential co

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: nto policy formulation, albeit at the potential cost of greater institutional complexity (see section 5.3.1 below) or a mismatch be- tween the range of objectives espoused and the resources available to achieve them. At the federal level, and to a lesser extent at the state level, Australia's two main political parties (the Liberal Party of Australia and the Australian Labor Party) hold divergent

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ia and the Australian Labor Party) hold divergent views on the energy transition and its social implications. Typically, governments led by the Liberal Party, often in coalition with the more climate-sceptical National Party, have been more reluctant to act on climate change than those led by the Australian Labor Party [68,71,72]. However, the state Coalition gov- ernment which introduced the NSW

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: e Coalition gov- ernment which introduced the NSW Roadmap held a more proactive stance on climate change than the federal Coalition. This may be attributable to the demographics of NSW, which are predominantly urban, and the fact that fossil fuel extraction makes up a much smaller share of the state's economy than some other jurisdictions [73]. The NSW Coalition government sought cross-party suppo

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: NSW Coalition government sought cross-party support for the EII Act partly out of necessity (as it held a minority in the upper house of Parliament) but also to reduce the risk that the Act would be repealed under a future change of government [74]. In the resulting cross-party negotiations, Labor and the Greens proposed amendments that resul- ted respectively in the expansion of REZs to two key

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: respectively in the expansion of REZs to two key industrial regions and the adoption of guidelines on engagement with First Nations people ([75], INT18R). The negotiations promoted coherence by ensuring that a wider range of equity objectives were integrated into the Act. Following a change of government in 2023, the incoming Labor gov- ernment reaffirmed its support for the Act but initiated a p

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: affirmed its support for the Act but initiated a policy review that aimed to improve Roadmap implementation through increased resourcing and higher prioritisation on the government's agenda [38]. Another review held in early 2025 found that stakeholder support for the objectives of the Act remained strong and that concerns mainly revolved around its implementation in practice [42]. 5.2.2. Conflict

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: s implementation in practice [42]. 5.2.2. Conflicting ideas of scale One common concern voiced by participants was that the scale of renewable energy infrastructure envisaged under the Roadmap is not well suited to the types of projects that would be most beneficial to First Nations communities (INT18R; see also FG01R, INT08B, INT09B) or regional communities generally (INT12N, INT17N): I think tha

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ommunities generally (INT12N, INT17N): I think that there's a general unawareness about the potential for community scale, community owned, community led energy solu- tions. […] the state governments […] say, well, we've got these five big coal powered generators, we need to replace them with big business renewables. That's just how the mentality goes. It's not, what about we have lots of smaller

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: oes. It's not, what about we have lots of smaller ones, which would actually make our grid more resilient rather than just a few large-scale ones owned here and there. (INT17N) Similarly, Proudlove et al. [76] argue that the scale of the National Electricity Market poses a significant barrier to the uptake of community-owned energy infrastructure. Numerous commentators argue that the urgency of th

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: Numerous commentators argue that the urgency of the transition instead requires an “all of the above” approach in the sense that renewable energy infrastructure needs to expand at all scales (see e.g. [38]). However, privileging some scales over others can come at a cost for distributive equity. 7 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c ie n c e 130 (2025) 104415 5.3. Institutions 5.3.1. Ins

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: c e 130 (2025) 104415 5.3. Institutions 5.3.1. Institutional capacity, complexity and inertia Institutional capacity across both government agencies and non- government organisations posed a major constraint on both the speed of the transition and the ability of those organisations to advance equity. When the Roadmap was launched, the NSW Government initially lacked the institutional capacity to d

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: t initially lacked the institutional capacity to deliver it, in part due to the legacy of pri- vatisation, the shift of institutional responsibilities associated with the National Electricity Market, and limited recent experience with con- struction of transmission infrastructure [38]. EnergyCo's capacity to engage meaningfully with communities was particularly limited during the early phase of th

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: particularly limited during the early phase of the Roadmap, although it improved to some degree as EnergyCo's staffing profile built up (INT12N, INT26G). Institutional capacity was also a concern for other organisations, including local councils (INT26G), project developers (INT14B, INT20B), Aboriginal Land Councils (INT18R, INT27N, INT28N) and community organisa- tions (INT23N, INT24N). Institut

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: mmunity organisa- tions (INT23N, INT24N). Institutional complexity emerged as a recurring obstacle both to the government's ability to coordinate Roadmap implementation and the ability of people to participate in decision-making (INT13N, INT22B, [38]; see also [42] and section 4.1.3). The complexity of the Roadmap framework arises not only from overlaying the REZ model upon an already complex thic

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: rlaying the REZ model upon an already complex thicket of federal/state regulation [36] but also from cross-party compromises struck during negotiations on the Act that established additional bodies for the benefit of workers and regional development (see section 5.2.1). 5.3.2. Institutional coordination Recall from section 2.1 that horizontal coherence (across policy objectives at a single level o

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ence (across policy objectives at a single level of governance) may interact with vertical coherence (across levels of government). A key insight from the NSW case study and related research is that a government's ability to achieve coherence between energy and equity objectives may be enhanced or impeded by the extent to which it can coordinate effectively (a) among different agencies at the same

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: fectively (a) among different agencies at the same level; and (b) with other levels of government. Horizontally, challenges to coherence arise in coordinating across departments at each level of government. Longstanding silos between climate- and energy-related institutions (and within the electricity sector, e.g. between regulating new transmission and generation) in Australia have constrained op

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: n and generation) in Australia have constrained opportunities to resolve tensions or identify synergies across policy objectives [36,72]). While the EII Act included some coordination measures from the start (such as the Electricity Infrastructure Jobs Advocate and the Renewable Energy Sector Board) it soon became apparent that a more extensive range of measures to co- ordinate across departments

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ge of measures to co- ordinate across departments was required, leading to the establishment of bodies such as a Roadmap Intergovernmental Steering Committee [38] as well as operational agreements between agencies and cross- agency reviews. Nevertheless, O'Reilly and Nemes [38] found that in mid-2023 a “siloed approach” to coordinating the transition continued to prevail. Different parts of the NS

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: on continued to prevail. Different parts of the NSW Government have given conflicting signals at times on issues such as planning approval and the suitability of land for wind farm development (e.g. INT20B, [77]). Vertically, coordination across government levels can enhance or impede coherence. Despite the intergovernmental coordination ar- rangements established under the National Electricity Ma

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ents established under the National Electricity Market, coher- ence across state and federal levels has often been difficult to achieve, particularly when different political parties are in power at each level (see section 5.2.1 above). Prospects for coordination became more favourable with the change of Federal Government in 2022 (INT13N). An example of where state and federal coordination can st

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ple of where state and federal coordination can strengthen synergies between renewable energy and equity is the National Energy Transformation Partnership launched in 2022 by Commonwealth, State and Territory Energy Ministers [78]. One of two priority actions in the Partnership's inaugural document was to “Co-design a new First Nations 7 J. Pickering and P. Chalaye Clean Energy Strategy, to ensure

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ng and P. Chalaye Clean Energy Strategy, to ensure First Nations people in Australia shape and drive the clean energy transformation.” The Strategy, published in 2024, complements state-level initiatives such as the First Nations guidelines mentioned in section 4.1.5. Despite these positive de- velopments, there have been ongoing challenges in coordinating the financial contributions of the Federa

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: dinating the financial contributions of the Federal Government and state-based schemes towards the construction of REZs, as well as varying regulato- ry requirements for community engagement across state and federal legislation [79]. 5.4. Interests 5.4.1. Energy industry interests The coherence of energy policies in Australia has long been hampered by the fossil fuel industry, which has persistent

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: by the fossil fuel industry, which has persistently (and often misleadingly) sought to portray renewable energy measures as under- mining economic growth, employment and regional prosperity [72,80]. Here we focus instead on how the interests of the renewable energy industry may influence the ability of the REZ model to incorporate eq- uity objectives. Solar and wind project developers have both c

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ves. Solar and wind project developers have both commercial and regu- latory incentives to engage with communities and deliver benefits (INT12N, INT14B, INT20B, [81]), some of which are heightened by the REZ model. Demonstrating community engagement and support along with a commitment to benefit-sharing is simultaneously: a criterion for obtaining rights to operate in REZs as well as planning appr

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: rights to operate in REZs as well as planning approval (INT14B); an important element in maintaining social licence (INT20B); and an opportunity for developers to stand out in a highly competitive sector (INT12N; INT20B). At the same time, interviewees pointed out that there are limits to companies' willingness to invest in community engagement and benefit-sharing, not least due to their foundatio

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: benefit-sharing, not least due to their foundational profit motive as well as government guidelines which set maximum amounts for benefit-sharing [59]. Competition between developers can be a double-edged sword when it comes to benefit-sharing. A representative of a First Nations organi- sation pointed out that this competition can be leveraged to improve outcomes for communities (INT27N). Howeve

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: improve outcomes for communities (INT27N). However, competition can impede coordinated and higher-impact allocation of benefit-sharing resources, as some developers prefer to ensure that the benefits they provide are individually branded (INT15B). 5.4.2. Interests within and across communities The extent of alignment between communities' interests in advancing the energy transition and social equ

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: in advancing the energy transition and social equity may significantly affect the coherence of REZ implementation. Community members have concurrent interests in securing benefits from renewable energy projects while avoiding their adverse impacts (INT12N, INT25N). One survey conducted in 2024 found that around 69 % of people living in REZs believed that “local communities stand to gain when thei

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ed that “local communities stand to gain when their local area hosts renewable energy projects if things are done right” [82], while another held in early 2025 found that people living in existing or po- tential REZs were less confident than other Australians that they will benefit from the energy transition [83]. A coherent approach is made more challenging by the fact that community members' int

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: hallenging by the fact that community members' interests may clash. Sometimes this may be a product of landowners competing to host a project or disagreeing with neighbours who wish to do so (INT26G, INT25N). Interests may also clash over how benefits should be distributed. The NSW Government's Benefit-Sharing Guideline identifies degree of impact and the size of affected populations as primary cr

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: and the size of affected populations as primary criteria for distributing benefits [59]. However, these two criteria may come into tension, as reflected in concerns raised by community members about larger but less affected population centres receiving substantial shares of funding. A survey of 147 respondents about the CWO REZ's Community and Employment Benefits Program found that over 83 % belie

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: oyment Benefits Program found that over 83 % believed that some communities should be prioritised over others in benefit-sharing, primarily for equity 8 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c ie n c e 130 (2025) 104415 reasons, while a majority thought that degree of impact (which pri- marily relates to smaller localities) should be the most important crite- rion [84]. However, respondents

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: important crite- rion [84]. However, respondents cited a number of different principles for distribution. A diversity of views may not in itself produce inco- herence, but the procedural avenues chosen to inform decision-making (ranging from surveys of the kind used for this program to more co- designed approaches such as participatory budgeting [85]) may influ- ence public perceptions of whether

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: 85]) may influ- ence public perceptions of whether the resulting distribution is seen as fair. 6. Discussion and policy implications The results presented in section 4 reveal elements of coherence and incoherence for both procedural and distributive equity, as summarised in Fig. 1 below. Some aspects of (in)coherence reflect findings from previous research on individual renewable energy projects,

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: research on individual renewable energy projects, e.g. varia- tions in the quantity and quality of consultation may influence the extent to which procedural equity is enhanced or undermined by the projects (e.g. [86,87]). The analysis also reinforces findings that procedural and distributive equity may interact to produce more or less coherent out- comes: for example, the extent of community parti

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: comes: for example, the extent of community participation in decisions about benefit-sharing and impact management may influence the extent to which those decisions align with public perceptions of distributive fairness [13,70,88]. Equally, the analysis also highlights the acuteness of the challenge of coherence in REZs in three respects. First, the larger number of proposed projects in a REZ inc

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: he larger number of proposed projects in a REZ increases both the amount of information that affected communities need to process and act upon, and the extent of institu- tional capacity needed to ensure meaningful and coordinated commu- nity engagement. Second, institutional complexity resulting from overlaying the REZ model upon existing institutions can inadvertently pose obstacles to procedura

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ions can inadvertently pose obstacles to procedural equity by rendering the system harder to navigate. Third, the intensification of benefits and impacts in REZs opens up new opportunities but also increases risks of inequitable dis- tribution. The intensity of these challenges is underscored by the fact that while benefit-sharing mechanisms have begun to flow to commu- nities in the CWO REZ, over

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: egun to flow to commu- nities in the CWO REZ, overall progress on the Roadmap remains behind schedule, partly due to growing community opposition [5], which may in turn have been affected by prior public engagement processes. The analysis in section 5 indicates that coherence and incoherence can be driven by a mix of ideas, institutions and interests, and that these dimensions may interact. For ex

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ts, and that these dimensions may interact. For example, institutional factors such as regulatory measures may change the way in which industries pursue their interests, while a mix of ideas, institutions and interests can converge to privilege large-scale, corporate-owned projects over community-owned initiatives (see also [2]). While it is difficult to draw precise conclusions about the dominanc

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ult to draw precise conclusions about the dominance of one type of driver over others, Fig. 1shows that some drivers affected multiple aspects of (in)coherence, notably institutional capacity and industry interests. Some respondents argued that interests ultimately mattered more than ideas or institutions (e.g. INT14B). Even if this view is plausible (which remains a matter for debate), this does

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: le (which remains a matter for debate), this does not mean that interests remain static or that ideas and institutions are irrelevant. While fossil fuel interests continue to obstruct a swift and equitable energy transition [89], the lower cost of renewables is shifting the configuration of industry and community interests, and our analysis reveals some potential to harness renewable energy intere

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: some potential to harness renewable energy interests not only to accelerate the transition but also to make it more equitable (see section 5.4.1). Interviews and documentary analysis revealed a range of policy options that could enhance coherence. While space precludes a comprehensive account of these options, a selection of key recommen- dations for current and future REZs is summarised in Table

Introduction Energy policy increasingly attempts to address multiple aims of en- ergy security, climate change and poverty [1]. While such policy offers pr

text: hange and poverty [1]. While such policy offers prospects for sustainable development and meeting climate goals, there are challenges such as land conflict and corruption [2]. In places like Sub-Saharan Africa, renewable energy projects are increasingly taken up in policy, thanks to Independent Power Producers (IPPs) and donors who provide aid for energy projects [3,4]. There is growing pressure f

text: energy projects [3,4]. There is growing pressure for both development projects and foreign direct investments to be conflict sensitive [5–7]. However, implementation of conflict sensitivity is lag- ging, in part due to the complexity of potential interactions between project impacts and conflict [8–11].
