text: n. The paper is structured in the following six parts. Section one 3 July 2025 ss article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). S. Park and O. Summerfield-Ryan articulates the concept of embodied political ecology and situates it within the literature on global supply chains and solar PV specifically. Section two details the methodology for the study. Section thr

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: details the methodology for the study. Section three then examines the global market for solar PV highlighting dependence on China and Chinese companies. Section four then maps the socio- ecological harms throughout the life cycle onto the global supply chain to document the embodied political ecology of the commodity. Section five sketches attempts to govern solar PV supply chains. The final sect

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: s to govern solar PV supply chains. The final section concludes with areas for further research. 2. Embodied political ecology Facilitated by decades of globalization, an estimated 70 % of inter- national trade takes place through global supply chains [11]. These are functionally and geographically fragmented networks led by lead firms that seek to find the greatest economic advantage in having pa

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: find the greatest economic advantage in having parts of commodities produced in stages using different suppliers, producers, and factories [c.f. [12]. Global supply chains have significant socio- ecological impacts [7,13,14], with analysis frequently focusing on spe- cific issues, corporations, and sectors to ascertain whether companies are meeting environmental, social, and governance standards.

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: g environmental, social, and governance standards. Many environmental and social standards are established by multinational corporations pre-empting regulation or to demonstrate their corporate social responsibility considering reputational risks from controversies informing shareholder and consumer behaviour [15]. A range of voluntary transnational governance initiatives for RE now exist with man

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: l governance initiatives for RE now exist with many driven by companies, states, and international organi- zations including the EU and the OECD, as well as non-government or- ganizations in a variety of multistakeholder initiatives [10,16]. Private or non-state market governance [17,18], includes an abundance of voluntary codes, self-defined standards, certification systems, and public reporting

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ards, certification systems, and public reporting [19,20]. LeBaron and Lister [8] reviewed 290 studies of the effectiveness of initiatives to govern global supply chains and observed that they often do not meet the criteria for minimising impacts even against their own metrics. This reinforces concerns that global supply chain governance lacks accountability given they are designed by corporate ac

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: untability given they are designed by corporate actors whose primary interest is not mitigating socio- ecological impacts [7,9]. Rather than focusing on specific impacts, this paper argues that commodities like solar PV have an ‘embodied political ecology’ or a range of socio-ecological impacts that occur throughout the product life cycle and therefore along the global supply chain. This is a conc

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: fore along the global supply chain. This is a conceptually driven argument that builds on concepts from the multidisciplinary literature on energy and supply chains such as ‘embodied energy injustice’ [21–23] and ‘shadow ecology’ [24] to recognise a plethora of socio-ecological impacts that arise across temporal and spatial scales from producing these commodities for global markets. The term ‘embo

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ese commodities for global markets. The term ‘embodied’ captures the idea from the building industry that energy and emissions created for construction should be counted in its impact, which scholars have argued should also include environmental in- justices as detailed below [21]. A solar panel, like a building, has embodied emissions as well as contributing to socio-ecological impacts such as ex

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ontributing to socio-ecological impacts such as extractivist violence, eco-toxicity, pollution, water depletion, community impacts, and forced labour, as documented below. We invoke the concept of ‘political ecology’ to capture these harms, where political ecology is an interdisciplinary field that recognises that environmental change is embedded in social, economic, and political interactions [25

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: n social, economic, and political interactions [25]. It investigates how economic structures and power relations impact socio-ecological relations across temporal and spatial scales [26]. Previously [24] used the concept of ‘shadow ecology’ to demonstrate how the international trade of tropical timber to Japan cast an ecological shadow over South-East Asian forests. ‘Embodied political ecology’ th

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ast Asian forests. ‘Embodied political ecology’ thus captures the inherent socio-ecological impacts of political and economic decision making throughout the life cycle of producing commodities like solar PV for a global market. Linking ‘embodied’ to ‘political ecology’ seeks to recognise the political and economic forces 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c ia l S c i e n c e 1 27 (2025) 104212 t

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: h & S o c ia l S c i e n c e 1 27 (2025) 104212 that shape the socio-ecological impacts of solar PV throughout its life cycle and therefore the global supply chain. Other concepts identify negative impacts but tend not to capture their embodiment in the commodity. For example, scholars invoke ‘sacrifice zones’ to explain economic and political decisions to contain environmental impacts and send w

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: isions to contain environmental impacts and send waste elsewhere [27]. The environ- mental justice movement used sacrifice zones to draw attention to the siting of extraction and polluting industries in primarily poor and people of colour communities, such that they had to bear the unjust burden of toxic pollution, waste, and chemical exposure [28]. However, sacrifice zones are static because scho

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: . However, sacrifice zones are static because scholars have examined the impact in a particular zone rather than embodied throughout global supply chains. Recently, Brock et al. [29] argued that energy is embodied and multi- scalar because new sacrifice zones may open along global solar PV chains, although they examine only one site. Others have identified three types of global displacement from g

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: entified three types of global displacement from global RE: dispossession, which displaces people and communities from land and livelihood through land grabs and resource exploitation; the pollution and degradation of ecosystems from the local to the global throughout the global supply chain; and resource dependence, or sys- tematic patterns of unequal exchange that lock states into “destructive d

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: qual exchange that lock states into “destructive development dependencies of primary extraction, land expropriation, elite capture, and unsafe disposal of toxic and hazardous waste” [1]. To date, this has only been applied to the extraction point. More frequently used is the concept of embodied energy injustice, which seeks to encapsulate the injustices that occur to people along the supply chain.

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: tices that occur to people along the supply chain. This shows that injustices to communities including environmental degradation often fall outside environmental impact as- sessments, occur across different jurisdictions and timescales, are disconnected from the sites of combustion and consumption, and are “hidden or removed from sites of political power” [21, p. 220]. Although they overlook envir

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: power” [21, p. 220]. Although they overlook environmental impact assessments undertaken at the extraction and production points, the argument is that the entirety of the global supply chain is not captured in the assessment of the commod- ities' impacts. These are defined as “transboundary socio-environmental injustices linked to energy policy decision-making” and include both quantitative measur

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ision-making” and include both quantitative measurable impacts such as emissions and qualitative im- pacts such as forcible displacement of people [21, pp. 220, 226]. They identify distributional, procedural, and recognition energy injustices with an inductive list of impacts detailed further below (see Table 3). In relation to solar PV, Mulvaney [22] argues that analysis of embodied energy injust

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: 22] argues that analysis of embodied energy injustice should identify the unit of analysis, from the solar park to the panel or solar cell. Recognizing supply chain complexity means selecting the material to follow through the chain. Here we seek to connect the socio-ecological impacts to the LCA cate- gories of extraction, processing, and manufacturing; installation; oper- ation and maintenance;

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: uring; installation; oper- ation and maintenance; and end-of-life of solar PV to the supply chain to ascertain how best to minimise them. Alternatively, Sovacool et al. [23] argue for a whole systems approach that includes multiple places and scales. This captures injustices from the use of smart grids to electric vehicles and solar panels. Using three scales from the micro- (commu- nity) level, m

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: hree scales from the micro- (commu- nity) level, meso- (national) level, and macro- (global) level, the research inductively identifies 44 injustices stemming from the energy transition. This enables the identification of four types of injustices: demographic; spatial; interspecies; and temporal [23]. The variety of injustices across the life cycle of RE technologies can be applied to different sc

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: of RE technologies can be applied to different scales. We add socio-ecological impacts from the LCA literature to the harms identified inductively in this literature. The concept of embodied polit- ical ecology recognises the power dynamics inherent in energy decision making for producing commodities for global markets at each point in the global supply chain, without necessarily situating impact

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: supply chain, without necessarily situating impacts in justice frameworks that stem from Western political philosophy [30]. We particularly wanted to document all of the ecological impacts arising from the extraction, production, use and disposal of solar panels to demonstrate the cumulative impact of their rise. Renewable energy 2 S. Park and O. Summerfield-Ryan uptake is positive for addressing

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ummerfield-Ryan uptake is positive for addressing decarbonisation but there are myriad socio-ecological impacts throughout their supply chains and lifecycles. These impacts should be assessed along the global supply chain [31], particularly because RE generation is often dependent on conventional energy, thus increasing its socio-ecological impacts [32]. More radical scholars argue that global cap

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: [32]. More radical scholars argue that global capitalist forces are contributing to an ecocide-genocide nexus [33]. This argument is that energy projects are often located in areas of Indigenous peoples and contribute to the further destruction of the planet through unsustainable production and consumption. The thesis highlights how capitalism contributes to global environmental change and the ma

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: tributes to global environmental change and the manifold injustices that stem from energy decision-making around the world. As York and Bell [34] observe, energy ‘transitions’ have historically involved an in- crease (in absolute terms) of the fuel source supposedly being transi- tioned away from, notwithstanding a decrease in relative terms. The necessity of decarbonisation means our current sust

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ecessity of decarbonisation means our current sustainability transition must see a phase out of fossil fuel use on the one hand, and a rapid in- crease in the uptake of renewable energy technologies on the other. We recognise the importance of going beyond an energy ‘transition’ which is primarily viewed as a change in fuel sources or a ‘technological fix’. Rather, there are underlying processes s

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: cal fix’. Rather, there are underlying processes such as a continued commitment to economic growth that also must be re-evaluated as part of a sus- tainability ‘transformation’ (for example, see [35]). In the following section we outline the methodology for identifying and tracking the socio-ecological impacts of solar PV through LCA and non-LCA studies before tracing them along the global supply

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: udies before tracing them along the global supply chain. 3. Methodology We articulate the embodied political ecology of solar PV via two ways: through the LCA approach which is often used in geo-engineering to capture primarily ecological, energy, and health harms, and through non-LCA studies that capture social impacts derived from a compre- hensive literature review, including the RE transition

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ve literature review, including the RE transition literature dis- cussed above. LCA studies use a standardised assessment framework to document impacts from energy systems (ISO 1440-2006). LCAs commonly identify four lifecycle stages: 1) raw material extraction, processing, and manufacturing; 2) installation; 3) operation and main- tenance (O&M); and 4) end-of-life (EoL) including decommissioning

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: nd 4) end-of-life (EoL) including decommissioning and disposal. As illustrated in Table 1which summarises our literature re- view findings, LCA studies have often focused on energy-related envi- ronmental indicators (e.g. Energy Pay Back Time [EPBT], and GHG emissions) [36,37]. This is important because it brings together a comprehensive list of all of the known impacts from producing solar PV usi

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: l of the known impacts from producing solar PV using this approach, and provides a baseline for identifying ecological and human health impacts of solar PV. This is detailed below. Although LCAs are useful tools to identify impacts throughout the lifecycle of a given technology, certain socio-ecological harms are difficult to quantify and are more appropriately documented using qualitative methods

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: appropriately documented using qualitative methods. For example, LCA's focus on energy-related metrics obscure impacts such as social dispossession and poor labor practices, including forced labor. Additionally, as DuPuis and Mulvaney [78] and Silvast et al. [79] argue, the use of models often conceals underlying assumptions and values which frame how problems are represented and addressed. Mulvan

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: how problems are represented and addressed. Mulvaney [80] observes these methodological limitations in relation to solar PV. We therefore supplement the LCA literature with non-LCA studies to assess a broader range of impacts across the lifecycle of solar PV using three databases: ScienceDirect, Web of Science Core Collection, and Scopus. We used the following search terms: “Environmental Impact”

The following table identifies the known inductive impacts derived from the literature on global energy and solar supply chains using the following search terms “energy injustice” and “solar energy injustice” and “climate justice” and “social impacts” and “solar supply chains.” One of the limitations of the method using these keywords was that it did not adequately ca

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: g these keywords was that it did not adequately capture significant literatures in political ecology, including from black [81] and feminist scholarship [82–84], and the extensive literature on extractivist violence from mining.1While many of the impacts are captured (see Table 3), including uneven distribution of project costs and benefits particularly by race, gender, and caste; dispossession; a

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: larly by race, gender, and caste; dispossession; and land use conflicts; our approach may seem ‘disem- bodied’ from the site of violence particularly at the point of extraction of critical minerals mining for solar PV. This is an unfortunate trade off in attempting to identify and trace all known harms along the life cycle of the commodity to the global supply chain. Drawing together the full impa

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: lobal supply chain. Drawing together the full impacts of solar PV into the concept of embodied political ecology builds on current concepts that are limited in how they apply to global supply chains, and the site specific and inductive approach they take to investigating their impacts. Embodied political ecology provides a way to capture all known socio-ecological impacts from all political and ec

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: socio-ecological impacts from all political and economic decisions along the supply chain, thus incorporating findings of site and stage specific studies into a broader conceptualization of the commodity. This helps to locate where and by whom such political and economic decisions are made, thus providing the basis for interventions to improve supply chain gover- nance. We hope the concept of embo

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ly chain gover- nance. We hope the concept of embodied political ecology contributes to building connections between grounded fieldwork and global value chain research. The benefit of such a concept is that it can be applied to all (RE) supply chains, with more work to be done identifying how and why certain decisions that lead to these outcomes are made. Next the global solar PV supply chain is documented before mapping its impacts across each stage, which reveals the need for greater transparency by China and Chinese companies, and more stringently governed global solar PV supply chains. 3.1. Solar PV's global supply chain By 2023, cumulative installed capacity for solar PV reached between 1581 and 1624 GW ( [114]; on comparing RE see Fig

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: 1581 and 1624 GW ( [114]; on comparing RE see Fig. 1). The bulk of RE investment has been for solar PV and wind [115]. Globally the cost of utility-scale solar PV declined 85 % between 2010 and 2020; RE is now cost-competitive with fossil fuels in many parts of the world [115–117]. This illustrates the importance of solar PV for the global energy transi- tion. This section outlines the market and

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: transi- tion. This section outlines the market and geographic concentration of solar PV supply chain to locate where these processes are taking place and therefore where the socio-ecological impacts occur. As shown in Table 4, China now accounts for most of the global installed capacity with a minimum additional 235.5 GW installed at the end of 2023. As demonstrated below, a similar trend is evide

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: 3. As demonstrated below, a similar trend is evident in solar PV production and manufacturing. Over the past decade, solar PV manufacturing has increasingly shifted to China from Europe, the US, and Japan [119]. This reflects China's more than US$50 billion investment in additional supply ca- pacity since 2011 and its focus on the PV industry as a strategically important sector [120–123]. This has

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: strategically important sector [120–123]. This has created significant geographical concentration (see Fig. 2), with many states relying on imported solar PV modules. Between 2017 and 2021 the EU imported 84 % of its solar PV modules; the US 77 % [119]. In 2021, there were 38 states with capacity for module assembly, although many of these were small or not fully developed [119,p. 26]. Despite this, China still accounts for 77.8 % of module production [119,125]. Ch

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: unts for 77.8 % of module production [119,125]. China is also the leading producer of many of the critical minerals used in solar PV [126–129]. As a result, China, and a small number of Chinese companies, increasingly dominate each component of solar PV manufacturing (see Table 5). China's dominance highlights several issues. The first is that supply chain concentration could limit the uptake of s

As the International Energy Agency (IEA) [119] notes: Based on manufacturing capacity under construction, China's share of global polysilicon, ingot and wafer production will

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: lobal polysilicon, ingot and wafer production will soon reach almost 95 %. Today, China's Xinjiang province accounts for 40 % global poly- silicon manufacturing. Moreover, one out of every seven panels pro- duced worldwide is manufacturing by a single facility. This level of concentration in any global supply chain would represent a considerable vulnerability (p. 9). Second, solar PV deployment ma

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: lnerability (p. 9). Second, solar PV deployment may be slowed by an increasing number of trade disputes between China, the US and the EU [130–132], supply chain bottlenecks, and financial volatility in the sector which has led to several bankruptcies [119]. Indeed, the escalation of present trade disputes between the United States and China (among others) demon- strates the vulnerability of global

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: others) demon- strates the vulnerability of global supply chains more broadly to growing economic nationalism. Third, the supply chains for critical minerals required for RE and battery storage are also concentrated and reliant on China, which are a cause of geopolitical tension (discussed below). [119] advocates diversifying supply chains, while Europe and the US have articulated the idea of ‘reshoring’ solar PV manufacturing [104]. The socio-ecological impacts of the current global supply chain are detailed next. 3.2. Embodied political ecology in t

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: etailed next. 3.2. Embodied political ecology in the global solar PV supply chain This section maps the four life cycle stages of solar PV to the global supply chain to document the embodied political ecology of the com- modity.3China dominates the processing and production stages creating significant socio-ecological impacts. However, the extraction and end- of-life stages also contribute to the

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: on and end- of-life stages also contribute to the embodied political ecology of solar PV. This highlights not just the need for greater transparency and accountability in how China governs processing and manufacturing, but global efforts required to minimise the impacts of solar PV for a global market. 3 Given the fewer socio-ecological impacts from installing solar panels, this second stage is co

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: m installing solar panels, this second stage is collapsed into the third operation and maintenance stage. 5 S. Park and O. Summerfield-Ryan Stage 1: Resource Extraction, Processing and Manufacturing: The solar PV lifecycle begins with raw materials extraction and processing [64,87]. Solar PV will require an increase in minerals and metals depending on the form the energy transition takes, includin

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: on the form the energy transition takes, including the growth of specific PV sub-technologies [59,85,92]. Materials used in the manufacturing of crystalline silicon (c-Si) solar PV, which represents 95 % of the market, and thin-film CdTe solar PV (5 %) are summarised in Tables 6 and 7 below. Some are toxic and some may also be deemed ‘critical minerals’ that currently have no viable substitutes a

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: erals’ that currently have no viable substitutes and have a high supply risk [77,119]. Supply constraints could create bottlenecks [87,119,135] for tellurium, indium, germanium, selenium, and silicon [13,85]. China is currently the largest producer of antimony, gallium, germanium, graphite, indium, and REE [126], while dominating the processing of cobalt, copper, nickel, graphite, and REE [136]. D

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: cobalt, copper, nickel, graphite, and REE [136]. Disputes between China and the US have already led to Chinese restrictions on the export of antimony, gallium, and germanium, reinforcing the vulnera- bility of the global supply chain [137]. Some argue that minerals demand from the shift to low-carbon technologies, “apart from some exceptions,” may be quite small over- all because demand may change

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: be quite small over- all because demand may change in sectors outside RE [45, p. 206]. Recycling solar PV panels, as well as increasing their operational life- spans, could alleviate resource requirements and reduce supply vul- nerabilities, thereby increasing industry sustainability [45,85,87]. The embodied political ecology of solar PV begins at extraction sites for critical minerals all over th

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: extraction sites for critical minerals all over the world, including the DRC for cobalt and the lithium-ion triangle in Latin America for lithium [139,140]. Extraction can lead to violence with people at the site being dispossessed of their land through evictions, loss of livelihood to make way for the extraction, and have increased economic inequality as a result ( [1]; Tables 2 and 3). Land disp

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: lity as a result ( [1]; Tables 2 and 3). Land dispossession frequently occurs on Indigenous land [141] and extractivism is “interwoven with… capital accumulation and neoliberal markets, which operate through state institutional structures that try to erase Indigenous bodies and experiences” [142,143]. Additional socio-ecological impacts include GHG emissions, toxicity, ozone depletion, radiation,

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: emissions, toxicity, ozone depletion, radiation, impacts on human health, land use, resource requirements, and water consumption (see Table 1). For example, scholars have identified silica mining in India being controlled by ‘sand mafias’ but extracted “by the marginalized and historically nomadic Kol tribe” for domestic PV production. In addition to the exploitation of the Kol, socio-ecological

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: to the exploitation of the Kol, socio-ecological impacts include water contamination in local river systems, noise pollution impacting avian and terrestrial species, and deforestation resulting in habitat loss and reduced biodiversity [110,p. 5]. The largest ecological impacts of solar PV occur in the production and manufacturing stage (e.g. [16,36,46]). This stems from manufacturing's energy-int

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: 6,46]). This stems from manufacturing's energy-intensive nature [58,65]. Although there are wide variations in LCA results, Weyland et al. [75] observe that the processing and manufacturing stage accounts for approximately 80 % of solar PV GHG emissions (see also [39]). The emissions generated from manufacturing is influenced by the electricity mix used, which is often from fossil fuels [37,54,69]

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: used, which is often from fossil fuels [37,54,69]. Fossil fuel use in the electricity mix leads to increases in respiratory inorganics and global warming [144,145]. Increasing the share of RE and increased energy efficiency measures can minimise these impacts [145,146]. The fuel mix is therefore a major factor in the embodied political ecology of solar PV. As noted, most solar PV manufacturing oc

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: solar PV. As noted, most solar PV manufacturing occurs in China, which relies heavily on coal-fired electricity generation [37,104]. Polysilicon is produced in Xinjiang and Inner Mongolia, while other supply chain stages are primarily located in Jiangsu and Zhejiang Provinces [104,119]. Yang et al. [37] observe, “while the export of Chinese PV modules supplied a large amount of clean energy to the

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: les supplied a large amount of clean energy to the world, it also caused significant environmental impacts in China”(p. 35) (see also [44, p. 1028]). Leccisi et al. (2016: 8) find that solar PV manufacturing in Europe records lower GHG emissions, acidification, and ozone depletion impacts when compared to the US and China, with “the Chinese production chain consistently show[ing] the highest 6 E n

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ion chain consistently show[ing] the highest 6 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c ia l S c i e n c e 1 27 (2025) 104212 indicator values.” These impacts can be reduced by increasing the share of solar PV in the fuel mix [54,65,77]. China is working to increase solar and wind's share in its electricity mix [147,148], which will decrease global warming, acidification, eutrophication, human toxicity

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ing, acidification, eutrophication, human toxicity, fresh- water aquatic ecotoxicity, and photochemical oxidation impact cate- gories [37]. However, China also accounted for 95 % of new coal power construction globally in 2023 [149] (see also [150]). The geographic concentration in Xinjiang for polysilicon production also has implica- tions for GHG emissions, as its electricity is primarily based

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: emissions, as its electricity is primarily based on coal, making this province particularly emissions intensive. The manufacturing of solar PV panels also requires the use of toxic materials and chemical processes. This increases human toxicity, eco- toxicity, and carcinogenic, and resource depletion impact categories [77]. Several hazardous materials are used in the dominant PV modules, includin

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ials are used in the dominant PV modules, including lead in c-Si PV panels and cadmium in thin-film CdTe PV panels (see Tables 4 and 5).4[151] recommend substituting toxic ma- terials to minimise socioecological impacts where possible. Solar PV records higher GHG emissions than wind and hydropower but is still significantly lower than that of fossil fuels [36,38,39,69]. Other impacts include water

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: l fuels [36,38,39,69]. Other impacts include water consumption, acidification and eutro- phication potential, as well as having occupational health and safety impacts, economic dependence, exploitation, job insecurity, and com- munity marginalisation [29,104] (see also Tables 2 and 3). For example, despite having less socio-ecological impacts and being touted as a means to achieve (an anti-racist)

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: eing touted as a means to achieve (an anti-racist) ‘energy democracy’ and ‘energy justice’ [81], the decline of solar PV manufacturing in Germany from competition with China is an example of how some communities became marginal- ised from the global competition for solar PV production [29]. China's role as a lynchpin of the global supply chain has also revealed forced labour as a human rights issu

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: also revealed forced labour as a human rights issue. According to official Chinese government reports published in late-2020, 2.6 million, or around one- fifth of Uyghur, Kazakh, and other ethnic minorities in the in Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region (XUAR) have been placed in state- sponsored ‘labour transfer’ programs to produce commodities, including solar PV, under the guise of poverty allevia

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: uding solar PV, under the guise of poverty alleviation [104,105]. The solar PV supply chain is particularly vulnerable to the use of forced la- bour, given the supply chain's concentration in Xinjiang, as well as the dominance of c-Si technologies in the PV market. Companies have been given significant subsidies to move to the XUAR and participate in forced labour programs. Examining the solar PV

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: in forced labour programs. Examining the solar PV industry, [105] iden- tified “11 companies engaged in forced labour transfers; 4 additional companies located within industrial parks that have accepted labour transfers; 90 Chinese and international companies whose supply chains are affected” (p. 7; see also Table 5). This illustrates how the PV chain is affected by forced labour, with companies e

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ain is affected by forced labour, with companies early in the manufacturing process directly affected by labour transfers. This, in turn, affects later stages of the PV chain, even if these companies are not directly involved. This is a non-exhaustive list, and for many companies their involvement in labour transfer programs lacks transparency [105]. Responses to forced labour are discussed in sec

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ]. Responses to forced labour are discussed in section six. Stages 2–3: Installation, Operation and Maintenance: Decisions that shape the embodied political ecology of solar PV also occurs at the installation and O&M stages although there are compa- rably fewer impacts than upstream stages. However, it does include land 4 Two main PV sub-technologies control the bulk of the global solar PV market:

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: es control the bulk of the global solar PV market: crystalline silicon technology (c-Si, 97.5 % in 2022) and thin-film PV (the remainder of global PV production, with CdTe PV representing the bulk of this share, [119]). C–Si technology is “expected to dominate the solar PV market for many years to come” ( [119]; see also [85]). For this reason, we exclude third generation technologies even though

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: exclude third generation technologies even though c-Si PV technologies are particularly energy intensive compared with other solar PV, resulting in higher GHG emissions and EPBT [41,61,69,70,152]. Higher emissions are likely due to the energy-intensive nature of silicon purification and crystallisation during manufacturing [61,77]. 6 S. Park and O. title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ion; EROI =energy return on en use requirements, potentially requiring trade-offs between electricity generation and other activities; noise and visual impacts; water re- quirements; and risks of damaged PV panels leaching toxic materials into the soil and water. The embodied political ecology of solar PV this stage extends to wherever solar panels have been exported, installed, and maintained. Si

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: have been exported, installed, and maintained. Size and siting matter in terms of the socioecological impacts of solar PV at this stage. Land use requirements for solar PV farm construction contributes to deforestation and habitat loss, leaching of toxic materials, as well as visual and noise impacts [96,153]. Several RE studies document that solar PV records relatively low land use impacts, espe

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: r PV records relatively low land use impacts, especially compared with biomass (e.g. [46,50]). In comparison, fossil fuels and nuclear power have ‘secondary effects’ on land use via site contamination and disruptions to local ecosystems [50].5The area required for solar farms can be 2–3 times larger than the area occupied by the actual solar panels [154]. Grid expansions to accommodate the uptake

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: [154]. Grid expansions to accommodate the uptake of solar PV will also entail additional land use, although Luderer et al. [64] argue that this “is small compared to the land footprint from the general electricity grid” (p. 5). Options for reducing land use for solar PV include rooftop and ‘building integrated’ PV (BIPV) systems [93]; floating PV [153]; and agrivoltaic systems [88]; as well as up

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: [153]; and agrivoltaic systems [88]; as well as upgrading or replacing solar PV systems at existing sites of electricity generation, which would limit land use and soil degradation. Although solar PV panels do not make noise during operation, installation creates some noise pollution, impacting local communities and animal populations [96]. These noise impacts are relatively minor compared with ot

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: oise impacts are relatively minor compared with other RE during their construction phases. Constructing and operating large-scale solar PV farms also creates visual pollution, which can lead to community opposition and project delays [96]. Ways to reduce visual impacts are the same as for reducing land requirements or using previously contaminated ‘brownfield’ sites. Smaller-scale community RE als

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ‘brownfield’ sites. Smaller-scale community RE also reduce visual impacts associated with utility-scale projects, and studies have shown that community engagement reduces opposition to RE [155]. Water use requirements for solar PV are also found to be minimal [51,66]. Although the operation phase is considered to have only minimal impacts relative to the overall life cycle, the use of toxic materi

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: to the overall life cycle, the use of toxic materials does raise the possibility of them leaching into soil and groundwater if the panel is damaged (e.g. due to reoccurring stress, extreme weather, or poor quality). Examining damaged PV cells in a six-month study, [48] find that both silver and zinc leaching were present, although “drinking water limits for [silver] were only exceeded on a few sin

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: imits for [silver] were only exceeded on a few single days” while zinc levels “did not exceed even the most stringent drinking water limits 5 Although toxic materials in PV raises similar concerns (see below). 7 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c ia l S c i e n c e 1 27 (2025) 104212 mpact Categories R GHG Tox LU WC Poll Noi Other Recycling x x x EROI Recycling regulation x Human well being x x x

text: EROI Recycling regulation x Human well being x x x Waste management x x x x x x Visual pollution x x ensity; Tox =(human and/or environmental) toxicity; LU =land use; WC =water nergy invested. at any time” (pp. 1674, 1679). Proper maintenance and monitoring can minimise this risk [48]. In the social science literature scholars have highlighted that people may be dispossessed of their land or acces

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: people may be dispossessed of their land or access to communal resources to make way for solar parks through “legal eviction, illegal expropriation (land grabbing), land speculation,” and losing their land rights leading them to become economically displaced [1,156]. In India, large-scale solar parks have created land use conflicts in often poorer, agricultural regions and have seen small landhol

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: , agricultural regions and have seen small landholders dispossessed of farmland, while poor households have also been restricted from accessing biomass and pastoral lands which disproportionately affects lower caste women [110]. Through the concept of ‘Cuerpo-territorio’ feminist political ecologists underscore the deep connection between land and people, where “[W]hat is experienced by the body i

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: people, where “[W]hat is experienced by the body is simultaneously experi- enced by territory in a codependent relationship” [84]. While using different ontological and epistemological frameworks, feminist political ecology reinforces the utility of the concept of solar PV's embodied po- litical ecology because it shows how economic and political decisions about energy shape socio-ecological impac

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ecisions about energy shape socio-ecological impacts on marginalised communities. Stage 4: End-of-life: Like stages 2–3, the end of life (EoL) stage in the solar PV life cycle is not limited to China, although it outstrips the rest of the world in installed capacity and therefore must grapple with disposing of solar PV on a larger scale. Indeed, the top five markets for cumulative waste volumes ar

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: e top five markets for cumulative waste volumes are China, the US, Japan, India, and Germany [157]. Mean- while some developing countries are already reporting environmental and health issues due to poor disposal [119,158]. The embodied polit- ical economy of solar PV captures how decisions about disposal con- tributes to socioecological impacts. Currently, solar PV panels are assumed to last betw

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: urrently, solar PV panels are assumed to last between 25 and 30 years; they can be disposed in landfill, incinerated, re-used, or recycled, and in some cases used for land reclamation [69,159]. LCA studies often exclude the EoL phase [61], although this is changing (e.g. [40,60,63,73,74]; see Table 1). Significant increases in PV waste anticipated after 2030 [157]. To date, most solar PV are sent

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: after 2030 [157]. To date, most solar PV are sent to landfill or are incinerated [77,95,151,159]. This poses a problem given their toxic materials (e.g. lead, chromium, bismuth, tellurium, cadmium, selenium, nickel, zinc, silver), especially if they are improperly disposed of which could result in toxins leaching into soil and water systems [40,48,63,94,95]. Incin- eration releases toxic and carci

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: 63,94,95]. Incin- eration releases toxic and carcinogenic emissions into the atmosphere [73], impacting human health and ecosystems [94,95]. Recycling is the preferable option [40,44,48]. The dominant c-Si technologies use hazardous materials such as lead and silver, while thin- film PVs such as CdTe uses cadmium/tellurium, which have different recycling possibilities (see Tables 4 and 5). text: ead [73]. Given the dominance of c-Si, Lim et al. [63] note that “treating and recycling EoL crystalline silicon PV are typically given a higher priority” (p. 2). Recycling can reduce environmental impacts when compared with landfill disposal methods (e.g. [63]), although it can be more energy-intensive and increase GHG emissions (e.g. [44]). However, Klugmann-Radziemska and Kuczyn´ska-Łaz˙ewska [

text: er, Klugmann-Radziemska and Kuczyn´ska-Łaz˙ewska [60] argue that recycling can help substantially reduce solar PV's overall energy requirements and reduce GHG and toxic emissions (see also [63]). Recycling glass can reduce respiratory inorganic impacts [77], while recycling copper and aluminium reduces mining impacts [74]. Recy- cling solar materials can have negative socioecological impacts. In I

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ls can have negative socioecological impacts. In India for example, recycling is often undertaken by an informal workforce comprised of socially marginalised groups including children, posing ecological and human health and safety risks [110]. Pre-empting discussions on minimising the embodied political ecology of solar PV through extended producer responsibility principles, LCA analysis recognise

text: responsibility principles, LCA analysis recognises the module design could also improve EoL management.6Currently, solar PV panels have not been designed with the recovery of materials in mind. This section has highlighted the various stages of the solar PV life cycle to demonstrate why and where the socio-ecological impacts are occurring. This better identifies how energy decisions along the life cycle shape the embodied political ecology of producing solar PV for a global market. The following section examines efforts t

text: l market. The following section examines efforts to govern the global solar PV supply chain to minimise its embodied political ecology. 3.3. Minimising the embodied political ecology in global solar PV chains As noted from the outset, voluntary non-state market governance is used to mitigate the socio-ecological impacts of global commodities, although it is generally ineffective in doing so. Gover

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ugh it is generally ineffective in doing so. Governance of solar PV ranges from technical and procurement standards, ecolabels, and in- dustry association standards to more stringent requirements including US legislation to stop solar PV made by forced labour at the border, and an EU Directive on waste. There are therefore a variety of ways in which the embodied political ecology of solar PV is be

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: h the embodied political ecology of solar PV is being addressed.7However, harder regulations for global supply chains to mitigate socio-ecological impacts remain rare [7,9,10] and may face greater difficulty in being established given tensions between the US and China, particularly around solar PV. This section shows what is being done to address specific impacts from solar PV global supply chains

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: pecific impacts from solar PV global supply chains, which only skims the surface of the many decisions that are made in producing solar PV for a global market that contribute to harm. Indeed, as the previous section shows, at each stage of the life cycle there are options for producing a commodity that have fewer negative environmental and social impacts. It establishes standards and certi- fication for infrastructure and the international trade of electrical and electronic goods. It has developed guidelines for meeting standards and certification for the performance o

While manufacturing will remain in China for the near future, France and South Korea have adopted standards and procurement pro- cesses to reduce the emissions intensity of solar PV manufacturing. Introduction Electricity and modern energy access are crucial for economic and social development, as reflected by Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 7 and its synergies with the other Goals of Agenda 2030 [1,2]. A lack of reliable energy is still an issue for seventeen million people in Latin America. A major reason for this is that most

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: tin America. A major reason for this is that most of Latin America’s energy poor live in rural hard-to-reach areas in Non-Interconnected Zones (NIZ), making it harder to connect them to a centralised grid system [3]. In Colombia, even though the country reached a 95 % electrification rate over a decade ago, approximately 1.9 million people lacked clean and reliable electricity access in 2020 due t

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: lean and reliable electricity access in 2020 due to living in NIZ, covering 52 % of Colombia’s territory [1,72]. Connecting NIZ to cen- tralised grid systems is often not a commercially viable option and not highly prioritized politically, which makes providing reliable energy to NIZ inherently difficult [8]. However, Colombia has extremely favour- able conditions for wind and solar energy product

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: able conditions for wind and solar energy production [4], leading several researchers to suggest decentralised renewable energy micro- grids as a potential solution to reach the energy poor in Colombia’s NIZ [1,5,6]. Renewable energy microgrids are a cost-effective, sustainable solu- tion to combat poverty and enhance social development [5,7]. However, effective interventions to diffuse microgrids must include th

Still, current studies on increasing energy access through microgrids in NIZ primarily examine the potential for deploy- ment based on costs [10], energy infrastructure [

This paper aims to address these research gaps by placing the views of current end users of renewable energy microgrids that live in NIZ in low-income commu- nities in a developing country first and comparing it to people in their neighbouring communities without such system

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: their neighbouring communities without such systems. This is especially important since the social dimensions of diffusing microgrids often determine whether an innovation is successfully adopted by the poten- tial beneficiaries [9]. The Wayuu people in La Guajira, Colombia, were chosen as a case for this study since they reside in a NIZ zone where decentralised solar energy microgrids have starte

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: decentralised solar energy microgrids have started to be deployed on a small scale [25,26]. At the same time, the vast majority of the region’s rural population still suffers from energy poverty [25]. The study aims to identify the specific enablers and barriers to microgrid diffusion and how the presence of microgrids amongst neighbours impacts the willingness to adopt them by non-users. The res

The article examines Colombia’s main rural electrification chal- lenges and legislations and presents the case study used in this research. 2. Analyses and discuss decentralised renewable energy microgrid diffusion in the study context and its implications for Colombia’s last-mile electrification goals. 5. Concludes the article. 1.1. The Colombian context In Colombia, one of the main electrification challenges is the rural energy poor’s geographical isolation,

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: s the rural energy poor’s geographical isolation, making connecting them to the national electricity grid inherently hard [1]. The difficulty hindering providing the rural energy poor with clean and reliable energy is that 52 % of Colombian territory lies in NIZ, making it difficult and expensive to connect them to the national grid [8]. The NIZ are areas which do not have access to the National I

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: e areas which do not have access to the National Interconnected System of energy (SIN), as seen in Fig. 1 and have an estimated population of 1,900,000 inhabitants [67], with a population density of three inhabitants per square kilometre [66]. Comparably, the average population density in Colombia as of 2020 is 46 people per square kilometre of land area [68]. This low population density and geogr

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: d area [68]. This low population density and geographical isolation make it technically and economically non-viable to connect these NIZ areas to the SIN [1]. Furthermore, as seen in Fig. 2, the density of the NIZ varies. Some parts of the NIZ have a much lower population density than three inhabitants per square kilometre, making it even harder to connect these areas to the SIN [66]. The people w

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: connect these areas to the SIN [66]. The people with energy in Colombia’s rural areas typically depend on diesel gen-sets that face an insecure supply [71]. Currently, 95 % of people with energy access in Colombia’s NIZ use diesel, and the remaining 5 % use renewable energy, driven by the high subsidies provided by the national government to fuel suppliers, reaching more than USD 33 million in 20

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: suppliers, reaching more than USD 33 million in 2018 [1]. The Colombian government aims to address this and has, since 2015, created an enabling environment for non-conventional renewable energy technologies [1]. Colombia has two relevant legislations supporting the diffusion of renewable energy technologies in NIZ – Article 185 of the National Development Plan (2014–2018) (Law 1753 (2015)) [27] a

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: elopment Plan (2014–2018) (Law 1753 (2015)) [27] and Law 1715 (2014) [28]. The first legislation sets up a fund for developing Colombia’s Pacific Coast [27]. The second legislation promotes the diffusion and use of renewable energy nationally, including in NIZ [28]. While Law 1715 was ratified in 2014, it has not yet developed the necessary regulations and thus far 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h &

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: w awareness of renewable energy amongst po- tential recipients and how to create reliable energy for dispersed rural populations [9]. Even with the shortcomings of Law 1715, it is a step forward in incentivising renewable energy technologies [9]. Especially since past efforts to electrify NIZ in Colombia have relied on diesel generators subsidised through a Solidarity Fund [9], which protects ener

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: through a Solidarity Fund [9], which protects energy users in NIZ from high electricity prices by subsidizing energy usage in NIZ by redistributing funds raised by energy consumption in higher strata [66]. An estimated USD 2.3 billion investment is needed to provide universal energy access in Colombia [74]. This is much higher than the current funds focusing on rural electrification, which amounts

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: s focusing on rural electrification, which amounts to USD 216,61 million annually, as seen in Table 1. Such an investment requires a legislative and institutional framework to incentivise electrification through subsidies and private investments and should prioritise renewable energy systems while discouraging the use of diesel [1]. Another important factor to incorporate when addressing these bar

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: nt factor to incorporate when addressing these barriers is understanding how communities use and think about energy and how this can change when increasing energy access [9]. Therefore, the re- cipients’ opinions are crucial for such a high investment to succeed, and a narrow focus on energy infrastructure is insufficient [9]. With microgrids serving as an effective strategy to provide energy to C

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: ng as an effective strategy to provide energy to Colombia’s NIZ, several such attempts have occurred in the past, with differing successes [29–31]. Numerous research groups have deployed microgrids in La Guajira’s Wayuu communities, primarily focusing on cost-effectiveness or optimal power models [29,30]. In July 2021, the Minister of Mines and Energy announced the approval of resources to finance solar panels that will power the homes of 4009 rural families in La Guajira [33]

Therefore, studies that compare different ways of accessing energy and the recipients’ opinions from a local approach are urgently needed to gain a more holistic view of the barriers and enabler
