This is particularly important to consider with the recent political developments in Colombia, where the newly elected President Gustavo Petro promises to prioritise renewable energy development in La Guajira while including the opinions of the Wayuus in the process [34]. 1.2. Where the desert meets the sea This study focu

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: .2. Where the desert meets the sea This study focuses on the rural indigenous Wayuu communities that inhabit Northern Colombia’s La Guajira peninsula, as seen in Fig. 3 [35]. Although Colombia has abundant and diverse resources, La Guajira is an extremely arid region that is only abundant in energy and minerals [35]. With some of Colombia’s and the world’s best solar and wind energy conditions, La

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: world’s best solar and wind energy conditions, La Guajira has the potential to provide reliable, sustainable energy to its entire population [36,37]. Consequently, large-scale renewable energy projects, defined as renewable energy facilities larger than 10 MW, and their associated transmission lines have started to be built in the region on Wayuu territories but only connect to larger cities [38]

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: territories but only connect to larger cities [38]. A major reason for this is economic; large-scale renewable energy farms are supported by multinational companies and the gov- ernment and have the potential to provide a vast energy supply to Colombia’s cities, which propose high economic rewards [38]. How- ever, these high economic rewards often come at the expense of the local Wayuu population,

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: come at the expense of the local Wayuu population, who do not receive the additional energy created on, or close to, their territory [39]. In addition, since many Wayuu people live in rural conditions spread out over La Guajira, connecting each home to a centralised grid is inherently hard [39]. The indigenous population is therefore left without centralised energy access and La Fig. 3. La Guajira

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: entralised energy access and La Fig. 3. La Guajira highlighted in red on a map of Colombia [62]. (For inter- pretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) 3 I. Granit Guajira is considered a NIZ, as seen in Fig. 1, since the people who live there do not have access to the energy networks that have started to be built in the

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: ergy networks that have started to be built in the area [39,40]. According to data from the Census of the National Administrative Department of Statistics in Colombia, 380,460 people are self- recognized as Wayuu [69], constituting nearly of half of La Guajira’s population and the country’s largest indigenous population [35,41]. The extreme aridity of the region, in combination with government neg

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: of the region, in combination with government neglect and lack of basic public services, has led to very harsh living conditions for the Wayuu people [39] (Figs. 4 and 6). While their region has numerous large-scale renewable energy farms and vast coal mines, 77.8 % of Wayuu do not have electricity access in their homes and face high energy insecurity [75]. Amongst the majority with energy access

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: rity [75]. Amongst the majority with energy access, most come from diesel-generated fuel, which has limited efficiency in meeting the required energy needs, is costly, leads to environmental pollution, and is subject to the regional availability of diesel fuel [40]. Therefore, these diesel systems are often deemed unreliable and unsustainable and require households to have enough money to pay for

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: equire households to have enough money to pay for the energy supply [42]. Such high rates of energy poverty amongst the Wayuu people contribute significantly to their extreme poverty levels and substantial water and food insecurity [5,7]. The situation has further deteriorated in the past years which, has led the Interamerican Court to declare the Wayuu people’s situation as a humanitarian crisis

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: Wayuu people’s situation as a humanitarian crisis due to widespread malnutrition and thirst [43]. A study focusing on large-scale renewable energy projects amongst Fig. 4. Wayuu shed used for animals or agricultu 4 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e101(2023)103132 La Guajira’s indigenous population showed that the Wayuu expressed favourability towards renewable energy if it fi

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: ed favourability towards renewable energy if it first meets their local en- ergy needs [39]. Decentralised small-scale renewable energy systems could however serve as a quicker way of reducing energy poverty amongst the Wayuus and solving many issues related to their humani- tarian crisis [24,37,44]. Renewable microgrids focusing on increasing water access by pumping groundwater would be especiall

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: r access by pumping groundwater would be especially useful for the Wayuus to solve one of their major issues – the lack of water [7]. With the diffusion of renewable energy microgrids having started on a smaller scale amongst La Guajira’s Wayuu communities [5,31,39], studies aiming to examine the enablers and barriers of their diffusion are however non-existent, which this study aims to address. S

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: non-existent, which this study aims to address. Specifically, this study aims to examine the enablers and barriers of microgrids that pumps groundwater amongst the Wayuus and how the water-increasing aspect of the microgrids is viewed within the communities. 2. Theoretical framework and factors determining successful microgrid diffusion in developing countries This study employs a modified versio

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: ing countries This study employs a modified version of diffusion theory as its theoretical underpinning to grasp the enablers and barriers of microgrid adoption [45]. The focus of diffusion theory is to determine the factors making the diffusion of an innovation successful. The theory consists of four main elements: the social system where the innovation ought to be diffused, the innovations facto

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: vation ought to be diffused, the innovations factors, communication channels facilitating ural materials in La Guajira (authors own photo). 4 I. Granit or hindering the diffusion, and time. This research centres around the social system, innovation aspect, and the communication channels used, as seen in Fig. 5. The time dimension and the entire adoption curve of diffusion theory is not elaborated

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: ption curve of diffusion theory is not elaborated upon extensively due to the time limitations of this study. Adequately including the time dimension would require a longitudinal study of how the adoption curve changes over time within the Wayuu communities which is out of scope of this study. This study defines innovation following Roger’s [45] definition as “an idea, practice or object that is p

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: finition as “an idea, practice or object that is perceived as new by an individual or other unit of adoption.” The innovation elements are often the most crucial underpinning factor determining whether the diffusion will be successful or not and covers five main characteristics. The first one is an innovation’s relative advantage [45]. In developing countries, especially in low-income communities,

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: g countries, especially in low-income communities, the relative economic advantage of an innovation is particularly important to consider [46]. With low pur- chasing power and limited financial opportunities, diffusing an energy innovation amongst potential adopters in such contexts is impossible if the innovation cannot provide an economic advantage to the recipients [23]. This is often one of th

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: ge to the recipients [23]. This is often one of the major barriers to renewable energy microgrid diffusion since such technologies often have high initial in- vestment costs [13]. Due to the high initial costs, potential recipients must have the extra capital to invest in the innovation and be provided with sufficient information about the full cost of adopting a renewable energy microgrid, especi

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: t of adopting a renewable energy microgrid, especially its low maintenance cost compared to fossil fuel-driven systems which can encourage diffusion [24]. Other impor- tant relative advantages in developing contexts are the microgrids’ ability to provide reliable energy and their ability to save time for the adopter [13,16]. An innovation’s compatibility with the potential adopters’ norms, values,

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: bility with the potential adopters’ norms, values, and needs is the second factor [45]. Microgrids, although of- fering clear benefits, often face many barriers due to failing to consider the recipients’ views, the inclusion of their norms in the diffusion pro- cess, and a lack of understanding from the recipients’ side of why the energy innovation is needed [14,15]. Another important factor to co

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: is needed [14,15]. Another important factor to consider when attempting to diffuse an energy innovation in indigenous communities is involving them in the planning process [21]. Neglection of the community’s needs and views often creates resistance to energy initiatives, leading to significant difficulties in the diffusion process and Fig. 5. The elements 5 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: elements 5 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e101(2023)103132 failure of the diffusion attempt [22]. Previous microgrid diffusion at- tempts in developing countries have failed due to these reasons. For example, in India, an attempt to diffuse microgrids in a new context failed to include the views of the recipients about the innovation, who claimed that microgrids provided fak

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: novation, who claimed that microgrids provided fake energy that was insufficient to meet their energy needs, leading the initiative to fail [14]. To avoid such scenarios, the involvement of the target recipients in the early stages of microgrid implementation is essential [15], especially to increase awareness of the system’s benefits [19]. In low-income communities in developing countries, such u

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: income communities in developing countries, such understanding is easiest transferred through direct observation and intrapersonal communication methods, leading to a higher probability that microgrid innovations are diffused [20]. A crucial aspect to consider when aiming to diffuse innovations using the sun and the wind for energy is the cultural worldview of such resources. For the Wayuus, the s

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: worldview of such resources. For the Wayuus, the sun and wind are important elements holding sig- nificant cultural value, which might significantly impact their view about technologies that use the resources to create energy [22]. The third innovation element is how complex the innovation is [45]. Training to acquire the necessary skills to use the innovation is crucial when trying to diffuse it

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: e innovation is crucial when trying to diffuse it on a wider scale within a social system [17,18]. This aspect is particularly important in developing countries, especially in low-income communities, where information is often provided by people from the outside with different ways of learning [24]. Creating an understanding and providing training on how to use an innovation is therefore a signifi

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: on how to use an innovation is therefore a significant aspect to focus on in such contexts, especially for renewable energy innovations that might be unheard of in such a sce- nario [47]. The last two innovation factors determining the rate of diffusion are observability and trialability. For an innovation to be effectively diffused in a social system, the potential adopters must be able to observ

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: tem, the potential adopters must be able to observe how it functions or try it out to determine its benefits fully [45]. The Wayuu people have strong networks and communal ties, therefore, the ability to have seen how microgrids functions in other communities are likely to be high if their neighbours have access to such systems [48,49]. According to diffusion theory, opinion leaders, the most infl

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: o diffusion theory, opinion leaders, the most influential individuals within a social system, are on the higher end of the social hierarchy and can greatly impact diffusion by promoting or disputing an innovation [45]. Such opinions are spread through communication s of diffusion theory. 5 I. Granit Fig. 6. Wayuu houses in La G channels which greatly determine what information spreads about the in

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: ly determine what information spreads about the innovation [45]. In low-income communities in developing countries, interpersonal communication channels involving face-to-face exchanges are often the largest and most important communications channel due to the low rates of information communication technologies (ICTs) [22]. In the context of this study, the solar microgrid serves as the inno- vati

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: tudy, the solar microgrid serves as the inno- vation and is given the focus through the Diffusion Theory analysis since the individuals participating in this study have only had such systems for less than two years, compared to the windmill systems that have been in place for decades. 3. Materials and methods This study draws on diffusion theory and follows a qualitative comparative case study app

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: d follows a qualitative comparative case study approach. The comparison is between Wayuu people without energy access, with solar driven microgrids, and that use windmill systems. A comparative study is especially effective to apply in circumstances that rely on multiple sources of evidence that need data triangulation, copes with a particular technical context with numerous variables, and where p

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: nical context with numerous variables, and where prior frameworks and theories guide the study design, data collection and analysis [50]. Three different research methods are used to underpin the comparative approach, namely ob- servatory research, semi-structured interviews, and focus group dis- cussions (FGDs). In total, 33 Wayuus from five distinct communities took part in this study – 17 parti

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: nct communities took part in this study – 17 participants in the semi-structured interviews and 16 different participants in the FGDs, as seen in Appendix A. Since semi-structured interviews allows for questions to be amended during the interview process and helps create and direct a fluid con- versation while keeping the participants’ focus on the research topic it was chosen as the primary resea

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: research topic it was chosen as the primary research method [53]. Furthermore, espe- cially important when working with communities with different access to energy, semi-structured interviews allow for possible varied views and knowledge amongst the participants to be included [51]. text: onducted in- person and five were conducted via phone. Seven men and 10 women participated in the interviews, as seen in Appendix A. title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion

Results and discussion The individuals representing the group with solar microgrids expressed strong relative advantages since they had acquired access to the innovation. Prior to the solar microgrids, they used to access water through either the older windmill systems or manually by throwing a bucket down a well and pullin

As explained by one participant: “previously when there was no solar energy we had a lot of work, but now that we have it, we have benefited from it, and we have not suffe

text: als.” However, although all respondents using solar microgrids reported strong relative advantages, three solar energy re- spondents stated that the new systems had only been able to give them a moderate level of water security since the water provided by the systems they used was not enough to use for agriculture. We conclude the paper with a summary of our contribution and a short reiteration of the avenues which require further investigation. 2. Climate justice, just transitions and inequali

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: 2. Climate justice, just transitions and inequalities Climate justice (CJ) and just transition (JT) discourses both share origins in the environmental justice discourse (EJ). While justice is a plural concept which varies across contexts, EJ typically treats injustices as “distributive inequity, lack of recognition, disenfranchisement and exclusion, the undermining of basic needs, capabilities an

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: n, the undermining of basic needs, capabilities and functioning of individuals and communities” ([20], p. 360) and in relation to envi- ronmental hazards and natural resources. Most scholars trace the beginning of EJ to the 1982 resistance against toxic waste disposal in Warren Country, North Carolina; a poor and majority African-American community (see e.g. [21–24]). Hence, environmental justice

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: (see e.g. [21–24]). Hence, environmental justice (EJ) is traditionally seen as a merger between civil rights movements and environmental groups in the USA.1 By the late twentieth and twenty-first centuries, EJ was adopted by urban environmental groups, indigenous land movements and socio-economic justice movements that rejected the conception of the environment as wilderness and entirely void of

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: he environment as wilderness and entirely void of race and class issues ([29], pp., 22, - 23, [30,31]. Indeed, EJ is known to incorporate traditional and structural inequalities such as class, gender, race and ethnicity in their analyses of wider environmental injustices ([32], p. 80). In the Global North, the EJ discourse was predominantly preoccu- pied with a) marginalised communities that were

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: - pied with a) marginalised communities that were disproportionately affected by environmental risks and b) advocacy for green spaces and parks ([20], p. 360). In the Global South, EJ has been associated with the various organisations and movements expressing resistance to the entanglement of capitalist accumulation and environmental degrada- tion, e.g. deforestation in Brazil and hydrocarbon extr

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: e.g. deforestation in Brazil and hydrocarbon extraction in the Niger Delta [33]. CJ can be seen as the natural outgrowth of environmental justice in the face of increasing evidence for anthropogenic global warming and ultimately represents the solidification of climate change activism in the 1990s [20,34,35]. The concept was first introduced to the international political arena in the Bali Princi

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: e international political arena in the Bali Principles of Climate Justice defined by Global South and North actors at the 2002 United Nations World Summit on Sustainable Development in Johannesburg [36]. Early iterations of JT can be seen as a labour-oriented version of EJ 1 Other scholars indicate earlier instances of EJMs such as the United Farmworkers of America's resistance against community a

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: orkers of America's resistance against community and worker exposure to pesticides in the late 1960s and 1970s [25,26] and the Italian experience with “class ecology” in relation to occupational health hazards in the 1970s [27,28]. 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c ia l S c i e n ce97(2023)102974 adopted by trade unions who were concerned about the impact of environmental regulations and stand

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: the impact of environmental regulations and standards on jobs [37]. In the context of climate change, JT recognises trade-offs between competing needs, e.g. employment and access to energy vis-a-vis climate priorities [7]. Ac- cording to McCauley & Heffron, JT then refers to “the development of principles, tools and agreements that ensure a fair and equitable tran- sition for all individuals and

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: nd equitable tran- sition for all individuals and communities” ([10], p. 2). Trade-unions and labour organisations following the JT discourse are joining con- versations on climate change in order to promote the labour-friendly transformations of productive systems into low-carbon variants (Mor- ena et al. in: Velicu & Barca [38]). Before discussing the approach to inequalities in the CJ and JT di

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: g the approach to inequalities in the CJ and JT dis- courses, a brief introduction to inequality is conducive. Inequality generally refers to the difference between individuals or social groups in terms of their command over resources and opportunities [39,40]. Many studies focus on differences in income and wealth, or economic inequality, since social well-being in capitalist societies is typical

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: cial well-being in capitalist societies is typically determined by one's purchasing power [41]. At the same time, the manifestation of economic inequality is tightly linked to both spatial inequalities across local, regional and international scales and social inequalities along the lines of class, gender, sexual orientation, race, ethnicity, religion and disabilities [42]. On the whole, inequalit

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: ion and disabilities [42]. On the whole, inequality can be seen as a complex web of overlapping attributes which often mutually reinforce the unequal access to resources and opportunities. Markkanen & Anger-Kraavi [13] point out that the incorporation of inequalities in the CJ discourse typically frames disparities in the com- mand over resources and opportunities in terms of differences in the be

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: nd opportunities in terms of differences in the benefits and costs associated with climate change. Indeed, scholarly works in the field of CJ analysis often refer to “double or triple in- equalities” [18,43–46] which politicise 1) who has historically benefited from greenhouse gas emissions and who should be responsible for its mitigation, 2) who holds the highest capacity to reduce emissions and

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: olds the highest capacity to reduce emissions and adapt to extreme weather events and 3) who will suffer the highest losses and damages as the result of climate change impacts [47]. In the context of international climate agreements, it is often accentuated that the Global North is more 1) responsible, has the highest 2) adaptation capacity all whilst estimated to be less 3) vulnerable to climate

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: lst estimated to be less 3) vulnerable to climate change impacts compared to the Global South [48–54]. Other approaches in CJ analysis focus on more local scales, e.g. urban, community, household and individual levels, and highlight how income inequality and marginalisation determine disparities in re- sponsibility [19], vulnerability and adaptation capacity [55–58]. In addi- tion, these analyses

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: n capacity [55–58]. In addi- tion, these analyses reveal how increased vulnerability to and the lack of adaptation capacities vis-a-vis extreme weather events tend to exacerbate income inequalities and marginalisation [59–62]. Finally, there are CJ analyses that explicitly focus on attempts to increase adaptation capacities through the implementation of climate and low-carbon transition policies (

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: on of climate and low-carbon transition policies (C&LCTP) and for the purpose of achieving emission reductions [13–15]. The aim of these analyses is to accentuate the impact of C&LCTP on multifaceted and mutually rein- forcing inequalities. Similar analyses are also apparent in the JT discourse which accentuates that a successful low-carbon transition must address concerns related to gender and ra

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: ion must address concerns related to gender and racial equality, democratic participation, social justice [63] and between-country equality. Where the latter demands the recognition and inclusion of economies in the Global South [64–67]. The examination of multifaceted inequalities in relation to C&LCTP constitutes the main topic of this paper. Instead of merely providing a summary of the aforemen

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: tead of merely providing a summary of the aforementioned contributions, this paper presents a critical engagement through the consultation of three literature reviews that address the social outcomes of C&LCTP. The contributions by Markkanen & Anger-Kraavi [13], García-García et al. [14] and Lamb et al. [15] argue that C&LCTP represent a complex rearrangement of the social, political and economic

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: arrangement of the social, political and economic spheres which in turn subjects said pol- icies to co-impacts (positive or negative social outcomes). To increase public support, prevent regulation rollbacks and ultimately guarantee 2 C. Dwarkasing Fig. 1. Conceptual meta-review on the social outcomes of climate and low-carbon Author's own elaboration. the success of C&LCTP, it is therefore crucia

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: ion. the success of C&LCTP, it is therefore crucial to prevent negative social outcomes and foster positive social outcomes. While each of the aforementioned literature reviews address a different set of social outcomes, their common characteristic lies with the treatment of inequalities as negative social outcomes. Put differ- ently, inequalities enter the assessment of C&LCTP in an ex-post fashi

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: enter the assessment of C&LCTP in an ex-post fashion even if they are present before C&LCTP are implemented or designed. Meanwhile, recent literature have increasingly shifted away from the treatment of inequalities as “unfortunate side circumstance(s) of capi- talism” ([68], p.18, (brackets added)) and emphasises that economic and social inequalities are systemic and entrenched in socio-economic

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: ies are systemic and entrenched in socio-economic and political structures [42,69,70]. This paper follows this line of thought and argues that the analytical consideration of inequalities as an outcome of C&LCTP is limiting in two ways. First, it denies an assess- ment of the way in which systemic inequalities grant certain groups more or less privileged positions vis-a-vis specific policy proposa

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: ileged positions vis-a-vis specific policy proposals [61,71,72]. Second, it restricts policy design to the prevention of increased inequalities while abstaining from efforts that challenge the existing structures that perpetuate systemic inequalities [32,73]. Notwithstanding, the literature reviews by Markkanen & Anger- Kraavi [13], García-García et al. [14] and Lamb et al. [15] offer valu- able i

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: t al. [14] and Lamb et al. [15] offer valu- able insights regarding the transmission of C&LCTP to a plurality of social outcomes. On the whole, this is done through the lens of various justice dimensions: 1) distributive justice, concerning the fair allocation of goods and resources benefits and costs, 2) procedural justice, concerning who takes what decisions and 3) recognition justice, concernin

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: at decisions and 3) recognition justice, concerning who is and who isn't valued [74–76]. In the following section we consolidate these literature reviews into a conceptual meta-review that offers an analytical alternative to the treatment of inequalities as negative social outcomes. 3. A conceptual meta-review of climate and low-carbon transition policies and their social outcomes In this section

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: olicies and their social outcomes In this section we present a conceptual meta-review of the literature reviews conducted by Markkanen & Anger-Kraavi [13], García-García et al. [14] and Lamb et al. [15]. Our systematic meta-review departs from an extraction of the following information from the literature reviews: First, we systematise the transition characteristics discussed in the literature rev

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: on characteristics discussed in the literature reviews. This encompasses 1) the specific field tackled by the respective C&LCTP, e.g. energy, agriculture, waste generation, biodi- versity etc., 2) the types of C&LCTP considered and 3) the geographical area in which C&LCTP took place or are planned to take place. Then, we systematise social outcome characteristics by addressing the type of 3 E n e

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: characteristics by addressing the type of 3 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c ia l S c i e n ce97(2023)102974 n transition policies. C&LCT stands for Climate and Low-Carbon Transition. Source: social outcomes and the transmission pathways discussed in the litera- ture reviews. Transmission pathways is a concept we introduce to sys- tematise how each of the literature reviews treats the specific

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: ach of the literature reviews treats the specific way C&LCTP lead to social outcomes. Our redefinition of inequalities as ex- ante instances is established through the deliberation on transmission pathways. The following subsections examine each of the questions posed in Table 1 and relate the answers to the conceptual meta-review (Fig. 1) found at the end of Section 3.2.1. 3.1. Transition charact

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: the end of Section 3.2.1. 3.1. Transition characteristics We find that the literature reviews limit their assessment of C&LCTPs to the field occupied with decreasing the carbon intensity of energy and transport systems. Markkanen & Anger-Kraavi [13] divide C&LCTP into three distinct categories: a) those that aim to reduce energy consump- tion, b) those that aim to foster the deployment of renewab

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: those that aim to foster the deployment of renewable energy and c) those that aim to develop and preserve carbon sinks. On the other hand, García-García et al. [14] make a distinction between proactive policies, aimed at the low-carbon transformation of current socio- technical systems, and defensive/reactive policies, aimed at mitigating the effect of climate change through a direct reduction of

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: t of climate change through a direct reduction of carbon emis- sions. While Lamb et al. [15] do not explicitly categorise the policies they take into consideration, one can easily distinguish between those aimed at reducing carbon emissions (transport fuel taxes, energy and carbon taxes and energy retrofit subsidies) and those aimed at increasing the presence of renewable energy alternatives in cu

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: he presence of renewable energy alternatives in current socio-technical systems (renewable energy subsidies, feed-in tariffs and the direct pro- curement of renewable energy). On the left-side of Fig. 1, we present a simplified overview of transition policy types by following García-Gar- cía et al. [14] in their distinction between proactive and reactive pol- icies. In terms of the geographical ar

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: active pol- icies. In terms of the geographical area, each of the literature reviews attempts to provide a global assessment of C&LCTP and social outcomes, Table 1 Information extracted from literature reviews. Question Element Transition Which field do C&LCTP address? Transition field characteristics Which types of C&LCTP are Policy type considered? Where have/will C&LCTP been/be Geographical are
